content:
## Concepts in Focus React JS Why React JS? Advantages of React JS Running JavaSript in HTML Creating Elements using React JS React CDN React.createElement() Displaying Element JSX Babel Embedding Variables and Expressions in JSX Nesting JSX elements Including External JS File in HTML
## 1. React JS React JS is an open-source JavaScript library used to build user interfaces. It was developed by Facebook. ### 1.1 Why React JS? - Performant websites - Fewer lines of code - Improves readability of code - Less time consuming - Open Source - Reusable code ### 1.2 Advantages of React JS - Easy to Learn - Large Community - Developer Toolset
## 2. Running JavaScript in HTML We can run JavaScript in HTML using the HTML `script` element. It is used to include JavaScript in HTML. ```html
``` Here the `type` attribute specifies the type of the script. To include an external JavaScript file, we can use the HTML `script` element with the attribute `src`. The `src` attribute specifies the path of an external JS file. ```html ``` When the browser comes across a `script` element while loading the HTML, it must wait for the script to download, execute it, and only then it can process the rest of the page. So, we need to put a `script` element at the bottom of the page. Then the browser can see elements above it and doesn’t block the page content from showing. If more than one `script` elements are in the HTML, the `script` elements will be executed in the order they appear.
## 3. Creating Elements using React JS ### 3.1 React CDN ```html ``` ### 3.2 React.createElement() The `React.createElement()` method is used to create an element using React JS. It is similar to the `document.createElement()` method in regular JavaScript. **Syntax**: ```terminal React.createElement(type, props); ``` **type** - Tag names like `div`, `h1` and `p`, etc. **props** - Properties like `className`, `onClick` and `id`, etc. Props are shorthand for properties. It is an optional argument. ```html ``` The `type` attribute value of the HTML `script` element should be `module` to run React JS. ### 3.3 Displaying Element The `ReactDOMClient.createRoot()` lets us to create a root element to display React Elements inside a browser DOM **Syntax**: ```terminal ReactDOMClient.createRoot(container); ``` The `root.render()` displays a React node into the React root’s browser DOM node **Syntax**: ```terminal root.render(reactElement); ``` **reactElement** - What to render **container** - Where to render ```html
```
## 4. JSX React JS introduced a new HTML like syntax named JSX to create elements. ```jsx const element =

Hello World

; ``` The above JSX element compiles to, ```js const elementProps = { className: "greeting"}; const childrenContent = "Hello World!"; const element = React.createElement(elementType, elementProps, childrenContent); ``` In JSX, HTML tags always need to be closed. For example, `
`, ``. ### 4.1 Babel JSX is not JavaScript. We have to convert it to JavaScript using a code compiler. Babel is one such tool. It is a JavaScript compiler that translates JSX into regular JavaScript. Import babel in head tag. ```html ``` ```html ``` - For JSX, the `type` attribute value of the HTML `script` element should be `text/babel`. - For providing class names in JSX, the attribute name should be `className`. **Differences between HTML and JSX**: | HTML | JSX | | ----- | --------- | | class | className | | for | htmlFor | ### 4.2 Embedding Variables and Expressions in JSX We can embed the variables and expressions using the flower brackets `{}`. **Embedding variables in JSX**: ```html
``` **Embedding Expressions in JSX**: ```html
``` ### 4.3 Nesting JSX elements The `ReactDOM.render()` method returns only one element in render. So, we need to wrap the element in parenthesis when writing the nested elements. ```html
```
## 5. Including External JS File in HTML Create a file `index.js` and put all the js code in it. ```jsx import React from "https://esm.sh/react@19.1.1/?dev"; import ReactDOMClient from "https://esm.sh/react-dom@19.1.1/client?dev"; const element = (

Hello!

Good to see you here.

); const root = ReactDOMClient.createRoot(document.getElementById("root")); root.render(element); ``` Now, we need to update the script tag in html. **syntax** ```html ``` the update html will be: ```html ... ```

## Concepts in Focus Component Properties (Props) Component is Reusable Component is Composable Real World React Apps NPM (Node Package Manager) Pre-Configured tools
## 1. Component A Component is a JS function that returns a JSX element. ```jsx const Welcome = () =>

Hello, User

; ``` The component name should always start with a capital letter as react treats the components starting with lowercase letters as HTML elements. ```html ``` We can call the function with self-closing tags as shown above ``. ### 1.1 Properties (Props) React allows us to pass information to a component using props. #### 1.1.1 Passing Props We can pass props to any component as we declare attributes for any HTML element. **Syntax**: ```jsx ``` ```jsx const welcome = () =>

Hello, User

; const root = ReactDOMClient.createRoot(document.getElementById("root")); root.render(); ``` #### 1.1.2 Accessing Props The components accept props as parameters and can be accessed directly. **Syntax**: ```js const Component = (props) => { // We can access props here }; ``` ```jsx const Welcome = (props) => { const { name, greeting } = props; return (

{greeting}, {name}

); }; const root = ReactDOMClient.createRoot(document.getElementById("root")); root.render(); ``` ### 1.2 Component is Reusable A Component is a piece of reusable code that can be used in various parts of an application. **Example**: ```jsx const Welcome = (props) => { const { name, greeting } = props; return (

{greeting}, {name}

); }; const root = ReactDOMClient.createRoot(document.getElementById("root")); root.render(
); ``` ### 1.3 Component is Composable We can include a component inside another component. ```jsx const Welcome = (props) => { const { name, greeting } = props; return (

{greeting}, {name}

); }; const Greetings = () => (
); const root = ReactDOMClient.createRoot(document...("root")); root.render(); ```
## 2. Real World React Apps Creating a real-world app involves lot of setup because a large number of components need to be organised. **Evan You**, the creator of **Vue.js** created a **third-party package vite** to generate a ready-made react application setup ### 2.1 NPM (Node Package Manager) [NPM](https://www.npmjs.com/) is a **package manager** with millions of packages, serving as a repository and tool for managing JavaScript project dependencies NPM Company provides a **Command line tool** which will allow you to **publish, discover, install, and develop** node programs #### 2.1.1 Creating a React app using Vite ```terminal npm create vite@8.0.2 ``` Then Give the app name, Select framework (as React) and variant (as JavaScript) ``` > root@123/.../.../# npm create vite@8.0.2 ✔ Project name: … myapp ✔ Select a framework: > React ✔ Select a variant: > JavaScript ✔ Use rolldown-vite(Experimental): > No ✔ Install with npm and start now: > Yes ``` #### 2.1.2 React Application Folder Structure Let’s quickly understand the core structure of the folders. We have 2 primary ones: - public/folder: Where we will keep assets like images, icons, videos etc - src/folder: Where we will do the majority of our work. All of our React components will placed here. The **main.jsx** is a starting point to the Application **package.json** JSON file containing the configuration for the project, including dependencies and other project details JavaScript files containing JSX syntax should be named with **.jsx** extension #### 2.1.3 Starting a React Application Install the dependencies and run **npm run dev** command from **myapp** directory ```terminal nxtwave@RJSIVIMLDB:~/components-and-props$ cd myapp nxtwave@RJSIVIMLDB:~/components-and-props/myapp$ npm run dev ``` You can view the application in the URL `http://localhost:3000` in your browser. ### 2.2 Pre-Configured tools The `create vite` comes pre-configured with: - **Live editing**: Allows **React components** to be live reloaded. - **Esbuild & Rollup**: These are tools used to stitch together a group of modules into a single file (or group of files) This process is called **Bundling** esbuild is also a **JavaScript Compiler**, compiles **JSX into Regular JavaScript**.

## Concepts in Focus Keys Keys as Props Users List Application
## 1. Keys `Keys` help React to identify which items have changed, added, or removed. They should be given to the elements inside the array for a stable identity. The best way to pick a `key` is to use a string that uniquely identifies a list item among its siblings. Most often, we would use IDs (`uniqueNo`) from our data as `keys`. **Example**: ```jsx const userDetails = [ { uniqueNo: 1, imageUrl: "https://assets.ccbp.in/frontend/react-js/esther-howard-img.png", name: "Esther Howard", role: "Software Developer", }, ]; ``` **File**: src/App.jsx ```jsx import UserProfile from "./components/UserProfile/index"; const userDetailsList = [ { uniqueNo: 1, imageUrl: "https://assets.ccbp.in/frontend/react-js/esther-howard-img.png", name: "Esther Howard", role: "Software Developer", }, { uniqueNo: 2, imageUrl: "https://assets.ccbp.in/frontend/react-js/floyd-miles-img.png", name: "Floyd Miles", role: "Software Developer", }, { uniqueNo: 3, imageUrl: "https://assets.ccbp.in/frontend/react-js/jacob-jones-img.png", name: "Jacob Jones", role: "Software Developer", }, { uniqueNo: 4, imageUrl: "https://assets.ccbp.in/frontend/react-js/esther-devon-lane.png", name: "Devon Lane", role: "Software Developer", }, ]; const App = () => (

Users List

{userDetailsList.map((eachItem) => ( ))}
); export default App; ``` The `index.jsx` file in the folder is considered to be the main file in it. So, a path like `./components/UserProfile` can be used instead of `./components/UserProfile/index`. Keys used within arrays should be unique among their siblings. However, they don't need to be unique in the entire application. ### 1.1 Keys as Props **Keys don't get passed as a prop to the components**. ```jsx const UserProfile = (props) => { const { userDetails, key } = props; const { imageUrl, name, role } = userDetails; console.log(key); return (

{name}

{role}

); }; export default UserProfile; ```
## 2. Users List Application **File**: src/App.jsx ```jsx import UserProfile from "./components/UserProfile"; import "./App.css"; const userDetailsList = [ { uniqueNo: 1, imageUrl: "https://assets.ccbp.in/frontend/react-js/esther-howard-img.png", name: "Esther Howard", role: "Software Developer", }, { uniqueNo: 2, imageUrl: "https://assets.ccbp.in/frontend/react-js/floyd-miles-img.png", name: "Floyd Miles", role: "Software Developer", }, { uniqueNo: 3, imageUrl: "https://assets.ccbp.in/frontend/react-js/jacob-jones-img.png", name: "Jacob Jones", role: "Software Developer", }, { uniqueNo: 4, imageUrl: "https://assets.ccbp.in/frontend/react-js/devon-lane-img.png", name: "Devon Lane", role: "Software Developer", }, ]; const App = () => (

If you encounter the **ENOSPC** error, follow these steps to fix it: ### Increase the System File Watcher Limit Run the following command in your terminal: ```sh echo fs.inotify.max_user_watches=524288 | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf && sudo sysctl -p ```

## Concepts in Focus Components Functional Components Building a Counter App Build a Static Version in React Add Event Listeners State React Hooks State Hook Maintaining Count in the State Updating State Decreasing Counter Value React Developer Tools
## 1. Components There are two ways to write React Components. They are: - Functional Components - Class Components ### 1.1 Functional Components These are JavaScript functions that take props as a parameter if necessary and return react element (JSX). ```jsx const Welcome = () =>

Hello, User

; export default Welcome; ``` ```jsx import Welcome from "./components/Welcome"; const App = () => { return ; }; export default App; ``` The component name should always be in the pascal case.
## 2. Building a Counter App **Steps** - Build a Static Version in React - Add Event Listeners - Update Count based on User Actions ### 2.1 Build a Static Version in React To start with, we have provided components/Counter folder and corresponding files Now, lets build a static version of the application. **File**: src/components/Counter/index.jsx ```jsx import "./index.css"; const Counter = () => { return (

Counter

0

); }; export default Counter; ``` **File**: src/App.jsx ```jsx import Counter from "./components/Counter"; const App = () => ; export default App; ``` ### 2.2 Add Event Listeners Adding Inline Events using JSX is very similar to adding Events in HTML We should not call the function when we add an event in JSX **Mistake** ```jsx|UGLY onClick={onIncrement()} ``` **Solution** ```jsx|CLEAN onClick={onIncrement} ``` **File**: src/components/Counter/index.jsx ```jsx import "./index.css"; const Counter = () => { const onIncrement = () => { console.log("Increment clicked"); }; return (

Counter

0

); }; export default Counter; ```
## 3. State In React, State is a built-in mechanism where we **store** the **Component’s data that changes over the time** When the data in the **state changes, the component re-renders** Entire Component will be called
## 4. React Hooks **React Hooks** are special functions that are a new addition to React They allow us to use **State** and other **React features** in the **Function Components** | Hook | Purpose | | ------------ | ---------------------------------------- | | useState()\* | Add State to Function Components | | useEffect() | Execute Logic after the Component render | | useContext() | Access the Context value | ### 4.1 State Hook State Hook is a built-in Hook that allows us to add **state** to the **Function Components** **Syntax** ``` const [currentState, setterFun] = useState(initialValue) ``` The useState returns an **array** with two values: the **current state** and a **function** to update it The **Initial value** can be of any **data type** ### 4.2 Maintaining Count in the State Here, the count is **changing over the time**, So it should be maintained in the **State** Import the `useState` as below mentioned snippet. ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; ``` **File**: src/components/Counter/index.jsx ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; import "./index.css"; const Counter = () => { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return (

Counter

{count}

); }; export default Counter; ``` ### 4.3 Updating State - The **Setter function** is used to update the state - It accepts either a **value or a callback function** as an argument If the next state is **independent** of the **previous state**, update state with the **next state value** ```jsx setterFun(nextValue) ``` If the next state is **computed** based on the **previous state**, we can use **callback function** to update the state ```jsx setterFun(prevState => nextState) ``` Here in our scenario, the next state **can be computed** from the previous state ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; import "./index.css"; const Counter = () => { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); const onIncrement = () => { setCount(prevCount => prevCount+1) } return (

Counter

{count}

); }; export default Counter; ``` ### 4.4 Decreasing Counter Value Now, Lets handle decreasing the counter value ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; import "./index.css"; const Counter = () => { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); const onIncrement = () => { setCount(prevCount => prevCount+1) } const onDecrement = () => { setCount(prevCount => prevCount-1) } return (

Counter

{count}

); }; export default Counter; ```
## 5. React Developer Tools React Developer Tools is a Chrome **extension** which you can use to **debug** a **React application** **Installing React Developer Tools** - Search Chrome Extensions - Search React Developer Tools - Click Add to Chrome Button Refresh the Page, if page is already opened to enable extension On installing React Developer Tools extension you get two tabs added to your browser developer tools - Components - Profiler **Component Tree** Shows all the **components** in **tree structure** Here, We can select the **props** of the **components** and modify them

## Concepts in Focus Missing Export Statement Missing Import Statement class vs className onclick vs onClick Attaching Event Listeners without Declaration Updating the State Invoking Event Listener Setter Function Execution Style Returning Multiple JSX Elements Modifying the Props
## 1. Missing Export Statement **Mistake**: **File**: src/components/Counter/index.jsx ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; const Counter = () => { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return (

{count}

); }; ``` **File**: src/App.jsx ```jsx import Counter from "./components/Counter"; const App = () => ; export default App; ``` **Solution**: **File**: src/components/Counter/index.jsx ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; const Counter = () => { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return
; }; export default Counter; ``` **File**: src/App.jsx ```jsx import Counter from "./components/Counter"; const App = () => ; export default App; ```
## 2. Missing Import Statement **Mistake**: **File**: src/components/Counter/index.jsx ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; const Counter = () => { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return
; }; export default Counter; ``` **File**: src/App.jsx ```jsx const App = () => ; export default App; ``` **Solution**: **File**: src/components/Counter/index.jsx ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; const Counter = () => { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return
; }; export default Counter; ``` **File**: src/App.jsx ```jsx import Counter from "./components/Counter"; const App = () => ; export default App; ```
## 3. class vs className **Mistake**: **File**: src/components/Counter/index.jsx ```jsx const Counter = () => { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); const onIncrement = () => { setCount((prevCount) => prevCount + 1); }; return (
); }; ``` **Solution**: **File**: src/components/Counter/index.jsx ```jsx const Counter = () => { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); const onIncrement = () => { setCount((prevCount) => prevCount + 1); }; return (
); }; ```
## 4. onclick vs onClick **Mistake**: **File**: src/components/Counter/index.jsx ```jsx const Counter = () => { const onIncrement = () => { console.log("onIncrement called"); setCount((prevCount) => prevCount + 1); }; return (
); }; ``` **Solution**: **File**: src/components/Counter/index.jsx ```jsx const Counter = () => { const onIncrement = () => { console.log("onIncrement called"); setCount((prevCount) => prevCount + 1); }; return (
); }; ```
## 5. Attaching Event Listeners without Declaration **Mistake**: **File**: src/components/Counter/index.jsx ```jsx const Counter = () => { onIncrement = () => { console.log("onIncrement called"); setCount((prevCount) => prevCount + 1); }; return (
); }; ``` **Solution**: ```jsx const Counter = () => { const onIncrement = () => { console.log("onIncrement called"); setCount((prevCount) => prevCount + 1); }; return (
); }; ```
## 6. Updating the State **Mistake**: ```jsx const onIncrement = () => { count = count + 1; }; ``` **Solution**: ```jsx const onIncrement = () => { setCount((prevCount) => prevCount + 1); }; ``` ### Updating State from the Function Body **Mistake**: ```jsx|UGLY const Counter = () => { const onIncrement = () => { console.log('onIncrement called') } setCount(prevCount => prevCount+1) return (
) } ``` **Solution**: ```jsx|CLEAN const Counter = () => { const onIncrement = () => { console.log('onIncrement called') setCount(prevCount => prevCount+1) } return (
) } ```
## 7. Invoking Event Listener **Mistake**: ```jsx|UGLY const Counter = () => { const onIncrement = () => { setCount(prevCount => prevCount+1) } return (
) } ``` **Solution**: ```jsx|CLEAN const Counter = () => { const onIncrement = () => { setCount(prevCount => prevCount+1) } return (
) } ```
## 8. Setter Function Execution Style **Mistake**: ```jsx const Counter = () => { const onIncrement = () => { setCount(prevCount => prevCount+1) console.log('count:', count) } return (
) } ``` What might be the Output? React does not **update** state **Immediately**
## 9. Returning Multiple JSX Elements **Mistake**: ```jsx const Welcome = () => (

Hello, User

You are learning React

) export default Welcome ``` ```jsx import Welcome from'./components/Welcome' const App = () => export default App ``` **Solution**: ```jsx const Welcome = () => (

Hello, User

You are learning React

) export default Welcome ``` ```jsx import Welcome from'./components/Welcome' const App = () => export default App ``` ### 9.1 Fragment The fragment is an alternate way to return a single JSX element. It groups a list of children without adding extra nodes to the DOM. ```jsx import {Fragment} from 'react' const Welcome = () => (

Hello, User

You are learning React

 ) export default Welcome ``` **Short Syntax**: ```jsx const Welcome = () => ( <>

Hello, User

You are learning React

) export default Welcome ```
## 10. Modifying the Props **Mistake**: **File**: src/App.jsx ```jsx import Welcome from './components/Welcome' const App = () => export default App ``` **File**: src/components/Welcome/index.jsx ```jsx const Welcome = props => { props.name = 'Ramu' const {name} =props return ( <>

Hello,{name}

You are learning React

) } export default Welcome ``` **Solution**: **File**: src/App.jsx ```jsx import Welcome from './components/Welcome' const App = () => export default App ``` **File**: src/components/Welcome/index.jsx ```jsx const Welcome = props => { const {name} =props return ( <>

Hello,{name}

You are learning React

) } export default Welcome ``` Props are **Read only**

content: **How to Use GitHub Copilot on CCBP IDE** This guide walks you through the steps to log in to GitHub and enable GitHub Copilot on the CCBP IDE environment. ## **GitHub Login** ### **Step 1: Register or Log-in to GitHub** - If you **don’t have a GitHub account**, go to [https://github.com](https://github.com) and sign up. - If you already have an account, log in using your **GitHub username and password**. ## **Login to CCBP IDE** ### **Step 2: Access CCBP IDE** - Once you're signed in to GitHub, open your **CCBP IDE environment**. ## **Sign In with GitHub Copilot** ### **Step 1: Open the Account Menu** - On the **bottom-left corner** of the CCBP portal, click on the **account icon**. ### **Step 2: Sign in with GitHub** - Click on **"Sign in with GitHub to use github copilot "** to start the GitHub Copilot activation process. ### **Step 3: Copy & Continue to GitHub** - A pop-up will appear, copy the one time code. - Click **"Copy and Continue to GitHub"**. ### **Step 4: Open External Website** - You'll be asked: _“Do you want code-server to open the external website?”_ - Click **"Open"**. ### **Step 5: Activate Device** - GitHub will ask you to activate your device. - Click **"Continue"**. ### **Step 6: Enter Code** - Paste the **code you copied earlier**, then click **"Continue"**. ### **Step 7: Authorize** - Click on **"Authorize Visual Studio Code"**. ### **Final Step** - You’ll see a confirmation message: **“Congratulations, you're all set\! Your device is now connected.”** ## **Confirm You're Signed In** - Go back to the **CCBP IDE**. - Click the **account icon** again. - You should now see **"Signed in (your GitHub username)"**. ## **Using GitHub Copilot** ### **Step 1: Open Copilot** - Click on the **Copilot icon** near the **top search bar** in the CCBP environment. ### **Step 2: Start Using It** - GitHub Copilot is now active and ready to assist you with code suggestions and completions\!

## Concepts in Focus Third-Party Packages Advantages Selecting a Third-Party Package Third-Party Package `react-player` React Player Props Reference
## 1. Third-Party Packages A Third-Party Package is a reusable code developed to perform a specific functionality ### 1.1 Advantages - Easy integration - Saves Time - More productivity with fewer lines of code - Better Error Handling - More Customisation Options ... many more. We have used many third-party packages like _React Router_, _React Loader_, _React Icons_, _JWT_, etc. **Node Package Manager** contains Third-Party Packages for React JS, Node JS, Angular JS, and many more libraries and frameworks. To know more about npm you can refer to this. ### 1.2 Selecting a Third-Party Package #### 1.2.1 Things to Consider - Users Satisfaction - Popularity (number of stars) - Maintenance - Documentation - Number of unresolved issues Compare the packages in the above categories and pick one for the application.
### 2. Third-Party Package - `react-player` NPM contains a **react-player**, a third-party package that provides a React component for playing a variety of URLs, including file paths, YouTube, Facebook, etc. **Installation Command:** ```sh npm install react-player ``` ### 2.1 React Player Props We can provide different props to **ReactPlayer**. Below are some of the most commonly used props. | Prop | Description | Default Value | | --- | --- | --- | | playing | Set to true or false to pause or play the media | false | | controls | Set to true to display native player controls. | false | | light | Set to true to show the video thumbnail, which loads the full player on click. Pass in an image URL to override the preview image | false | | width | Set the width of the player | 640px | | height | Set the height of the player | 360px | | className | Add the custom styles to ReactPlayer | - | **Example:** **File:** src/App.js ```jsx import VideoPlayer from './components/VideoPlayer' import './App.css' const App = () => export default App ``` **File:** src/components/VideoPlayer/index.js ```jsx import ReactPlayer from 'react-player' import './index.css' const videoURL = 'https://youtu.be/YE7VzlLtp-4' const VideoPlayer = () => (

Video Player

) export default VideoPlayer ``` ReactPlayer Supports different types of URLs. ### 2.1.1 controls Prop **File:** src/components/VideoPlayer/index.js ```jsx const VideoPlayer = () => (

Video Player

) export default VideoPlayer ``` ### 2.1.2 playing Prop **File:** src/components/VideoPlayer/index.js ```jsx import ReactPlayer from 'react-player' ... const videoURL = 'https://youtu.be/YE7VzlLtp-4' const VideoPlayer = () => (

Video Player

## Concepts in Focus Third-Party Package `recharts` Advantages Bar Chart Components in Bar Chart ResponsiveContainer XAxis YAxis Legend Bar Pie Chart Components in Pie Chart Pie Cell Reference
## 1. Third-Party Package `recharts` NPM contains **recharts**, a third-party package to display charts in your application. It supports different types of charts like: - Bar Chart - Pie Chart - Area Chart - Composed Chart, etc. It supports different types of visualization methods like: **Cartesian**: - Area - Bar - Line, etc. **Polar**: - Pie - Radar - Radial Bar **Installation Command**: ```n npm install recharts ``` ### 1.1 Advantages - Responsive - Built for React, from scratch - Customizable
## 2. Bar Chart The **BarChart** Component represents the container of the Bar Chart. **Example**: ```jsx import { BarChart, Bar, XAxis, YAxis, Legend, ResponsiveContainer, } from "recharts" const data = [ { group_name: "Group A", boys: 200, girls: 400, }, { group_name: "Group B", boys: 3000, girls: 500, }, { group_name: "Group C", boys: 1000, girls: 1500, }, { group_name: "Group D", boys: 700, girls: 1200, }, ] const App = () => { const DataFormatter = (number) => { if (number > 1000) { return `${(number / 1000).toString()}k` } return number.toString() } return ( ) } export default App ``` **Output**:
## 3. Components in Bar Chart The `recharts` supports different Components for the Bar Chart. Below are some of the most commonly used Components. ### 3.1 ResponsiveContainer It is a container Component to make charts adapt to the size of the parent container. **Props**: We can provide different props to the ReactJS **ResponsiveContainer** Component. Below are some of the most commonly used props. | Prop | Default Value | | ------ | ------------------------------------------------- | | width | '100%' (value can be percentage string or number) | | height | '100%' (value can be percentage string or number) | One of the props `width` and `height` should be a percentage string in the `ResponsiveContainer` Component. ### 3.2 XAxis The **XAxis** Component represents the X-Axis of a Chart. **Props**: We can provide different props to the ReactJS **XAxis** Component. Below are some of the most commonly used props. | Prop | Description | Default Value | | ------------- | -------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | dataKey | The key of the object in `data` that we want to display it's value on the axis | No default value (value can be string or number) | | tick | Represents a tick | No default value. If false - No ticks will be drawn, object - Configuration of ticks, React element - Custom react element for drawing ticks (value can be boolean, object or React element) | | tickFormatter | The formatter function of tick | No default value (Function) | **Example - tickFormatter**: If we want to show the thousands in the form of `k` on the tick, the formatter function would be: ```jsx const DataFormatter = (number) => { if (number > 1000) { return `${(number / 1000).toString()}k` } return number.toString() } ``` ### 3.3 YAxis The **YAxis** Component represents the Y-Axis of a Chart. The Props of the **YAxis** Component are similar to the **XAxis** Component. ### 3.4 Legend The **Legend** Component represents the legend of a Chart. By default, the content of the legend is generated by the name of `Line`, `Bar`, `Area`, etc. If no name has been set, the prop `dataKey` is used to generate the content of the legend. **Props**: We can provide different props to the ReactJS **Legend** Component. Below are some of the most commonly used props. | Prop | Description | Default Value | | ------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | | iconType | The type of icon in the legend item | No default value (value can be 'line', 'plainline', 'square', 'rect', 'circle', 'cross', 'diamond','star', 'triangle', or 'wye') | | layout | The layout of legend items | 'horizontal' (value can be 'horizontal' or 'vertical') | | verticalAlign | The alignment of legend items in vertical direction | 'middle' (value can be 'top', 'middle', or 'bottom') | | align | The alignment of legend items in horizontal direction | 'center' (value can be 'left', 'center', or 'right') | | wrapperStyle | The style of the legend container | No default value (value can be React Inline styles) | ### 3.5 Bar The **Bar** Component represents a bar in the Chart. **Props**: We can provide different props to the ReactJS **Bar** Component. Below are some of the most commonly used props. | Prop | Description | Default Value | | ------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | | dataKey | The key of the object in `data` that we want to display it's value | No default value (value can be string or number) | | name | The name of the bar | No default value (value can be string or number) | | fill | The color to fill the rectangle in a bar | (value can be given color in hexCode or string format) | | barSize | The width or height of the bar | No default value (value can be number) | The value of the prop `name` is used in tooltip and legend to represent a bar/pie. If no value was set, the value of `dataKey` will be used alternatively.
## 4. PieChart The **PieChart** Component represents the container of the Pie Chart. **Example**: ```jsx import { PieChart, Pie, Legend, Cell, ResponsiveContainer } from "recharts" const data = [ { count: 809680, language: "Telugu", }, { count: 4555697, language: "Hindi", }, { count: 12345657, language: "English", }, ] const App = () => { return ( ) } export default App ``` **Output**:
## 5. Components in Pie Chart The `recharts` supports different Components for the Bar Chart. Below are some of the most commonly used Components. ### 5.1 Pie The **Pie** Component represents a pie in the Chart. **Props**: We can provide different props to the ReactJS **Pie** Component. Below are some of the most commonly used props. | Prop | Description | Default Value | | ----------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | cx | The x-axis coordinate of a center point | '50%'. If set a percentage, the final value is obtained by multiplying the percentage of container width (value can be percentage string or number) | | cy | The y-axis coordinate of a center point | '50%'. If set a percentage, the final value is obtained by multiplying the percentage of container height (value can be percentage string or number) | | data | The source data in which each element is an object | No default value (value can be Array) | | startAngle | The start angle of the first sector | 0 (value can be number) | | endAngle | The end angle of the last sector, which should be unequal to startAngle | 360 (value can be number) | | innerRadius | The inner radius of all the sectors | 0 (value can be percentage or number) | | OuterRadius | The outer radius of all the sectors | 0 (value can be percentage or number) | | dataKey | The key of the object in `data` that we want to display it's value on the sector | No default value | If a percentage is set to the props `innerRadius` or `outerRadius`, the final value is obtained by multiplying the percentage of `maxRadius` which is calculated by the `width`, `height`, `cx`, and `cy`. ### 5.2 Cell The **Cell** Component represents the cell of a Chart. It can be wrapped by a `Pie`, `Bar`, or `RadialBar` Components to specify the attributes of each child. **Props**: We can provide different props to the ReactJS **Cell** Component. Below are some of the most commonly used props. | Prop | Description | Default Value | | ---- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | name | The name of the cell | No default value (can be a string. This value can be taken as the content of the legend) | | fill | The color to fill the cell | (value can be any color in hexCode or string format) | The `ResponsiveContainer` and `Legend` Components in Pie Chart are similar to the Components in Bar Chart.
## 6. Reference To know more about the `recharts`, you can refer to [this](https://recharts.org/en-US/api).

## Concepts in Focus Prop Drilling React Context Creating Context Context Properties How to Access the Value? Consumer Component Understanding Syntax Windows App Example Initial Code Final Code Google Translator
## 1. Prop Drilling **Props** are passed from one Component to another Component that does not need the data but only helps in passing it through the tree is called **Prop Drilling**.
## 2. React Context **Context** is a mechanism that provides different Components and allows us to pass data without doing prop drilling. ### 2.1 Creating Context **Syntax**: ```jsx React.createContext(INITIAL_VALUE) ``` It returns an object with different properties to update and access values from the context. ### 2.2 Context Properties The **Context** object provides two properties. 1. Consumer 2. Provider ### 2.3 How to Access the Value? We can access the value in the **Context** using `Consumer Component` provided by the Context Object.
## 3. Consumer Component **Syntax**: ```jsx {/*callback function*/} ``` - We access the `Consumer component` using dot notation from the `context object`. - We will give a `callback function` as children for `Consumer Component`. ### 3.1 Understanding Syntax The callback function receives the current **context value** as an argument and returns a **component** or a **JSX element**.
## 4. Windows App Example ### 4.1 Initial Code Run the below command in your IDE to download the initial code. ccbp start RJSIVWKZP2 ### 4.2 Final Code Run the below command in your IDE to download the final code. ccbp start RJSIVM06DJ

## 1. Provider - **Provider** Component updates the value of Context. - When the provider updates context, all the nested consumers of that provider will be **re-rendered**. - Updated context value can only be accessed by the consumers nested within the provider.

## Concepts in Focus Conditional Rendering Using an If...Else Statement Using Element Variables Using Ternary Operators Using Logical && Operator Common Mistake
## 1 . Conditional Rendering **Conditional Rendering** allows us to render different elements or components based on a condition. Different ways to implement **Conditional Rendering** are: - Using an If...Else Statement - Using Element Variables - Using Ternary Operators - Using Logical && Operator Lets take an example and understand this. Consider the below inital codes. **File**: src/App.jsx ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; import Welcome from "./components/Welcome"; import "./App.css"; const App = () => { const [isLoggedIn, setIsLoggedIn] = useState(true); return (

Hello, User

); }; export default App; ``` **File**: src/components/Welcome.jsx ```jsx import "./index.css"; const Welcome = (props) => { const { name, greeting } = props; return (

{greeting}, {name}

); }; export default Welcome; ``` ### 1.1 Using an If...Else Statement ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; import Welcome from "./components/Welcome"; import "./App.css"; const App = () => { const [isLoggedIn, setIsLoggedIn] = useState(true); const renderAuthButton = () => { if (isLoggedIn === true) { return ; } return ; }; return (
{renderAuthButton()}
); }; export default App; ``` ### 1.2 Using Element Variables ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; import Welcome from "./components/Welcome"; import "./App.css"; const App = () => { const [isLoggedIn, setIsLoggedIn] = useState(true); let authButton; if (isLoggedIn === true) { authButton = ; } else { authButton = ; } return (
{authButton}
); }; export default App; ``` ### 1.3 Using Ternary Operators ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; import Welcome from "./components/Welcome"; import "./App.css"; const App = () => { const [isLoggedIn, setIsLoggedIn] = useState(true); return (
{isLoggedIn ? : }
); }; export default App; ``` ### 1.4 Using Logical && Operator ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; import Welcome from "./components/Welcome"; import "./App.css"; const App = () => { const [isLoggedIn, setIsLoggedIn] = useState(true); return (
{isLoggedIn && } {!isLoggedIn && }
); }; export default App; ``` Conditional Rendering can be achieved using inline styles or adding classes with CSS `display` property with value `none`. However, it is not preferable.
## 2. Common Mistake Prop name not being passed accurately for any React Component **Example**: **File**: src/components/Welcome/index.jsx ```jsx import "./index.css"; const Welcome = (props) => { const { name, greetings } = props; return (

{greetings}, {name}

); }; export default Welcome; ``` **File**: src/App.jsx ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; import Welcome from "./components/Welcome"; import "./App.css"; const App = () => { const [isLoggedIn, setIsLoggedIn] = useState(true); return (
{isLoggedIn && } {!isLoggedIn && }
); }; export default App; ``` As you can see in the above example, In App.jsx, while passing the prop, we are giving the prop name as `greeting` But, In the component, we are accessing the prop we are accessing it as `greetings` So, when we execute it, the `greetings` will have the value as `undefined` So, It is very important that the **props that you pass and the props that you access**, have the same name.

unit_title: Debugging using Developer tools | Reading Material
content: ## Debugging Debugging is the process of finding & fixing the bugs in the code. We can debug using: - Browser Developer Tools - React Developer Tools ### Browser Developer Tools These are the tools provided by the browsers to debug the application loaded in the web browser. Using Browser Developer Tools, we can: - View the source code (HTML, CSS, and JS) - View and change CSS - View logged messages in the console - Run JavaScript in the console - Check the responsiveness of an application, etc. ### React Developer Tools For React Developer Tools, we can install the React Developer Tools Extension for Google Chrome.

## Concepts in Focus Third-Party Packages Installing UUID Importing of UUID Best Practice Updating a Property of an Item inside List Contacts App Final Code
## 1. Third-Party Packages - UUID (Universally Unique IDentifier) - Using the UUID package, we can generate a unique id ### 1.1 Installing UUID ```Terminal npm install uuid ``` ### 1.2 Importing of UUID **UUID** package provides **uuidv4()**, which returns a unique id whenever it is called. ```jsx import {v4 as uuidv4} from 'uuid' ```
## 2. Best Practice - The **state** should be **immutable**. We shouldn’t update the array/object directly. - The best practice is to create a new array/object from the array/object in the previous state using the **spread** operator. ```js|UGLY contactsList = initialContactsList ``` ```js|UGLY contactsList.push(...) ``` ```js|CLEAN setContactsList(prevContactsList => [...prevContactsList, newContact]) ``` ### 2.1 Updating a Property of an Item inside List We should not update the property of a list item directly. To update the property of a list item, we should create a new object and return it to the list. **Syntax**: ```Terminal {...object, newItem} ```
## 3. Contacts App Final Code **File: src/App.jsx** ```jsx import {useState} from 'react' import {v4 as uuidv4} from 'uuid' import ContactItem from './components/ContactItem' import './App.css' const initialContactsList = [ { id: uuidv4(), name: 'Ram', mobileNo: 9999988888, isFavorite: false, }, { id: uuidv4(), name: 'Pavan', mobileNo: 8888866666, isFavorite: true, }, { id: uuidv4(), name: 'Nikhil', mobileNo: 9999955555, isFavorite: false, }, ] const App = () => { const [contactsList, setContactsList] = useState(initialContactsList) const [name, setName] = useState('') const [mobileNo, setMobileNo] = useState('') const toggleIsFavorite = id => { setContactsList(prevContactsList => { return prevContactsList.map(eachContact => { if (id === eachContact.id) { return {...eachContact, isFavorite: !eachContact.isFavorite} } return eachContact }) }) } const onAddContact = event => { event.preventDefault() const newContact = { id: uuidv4(), name, mobileNo, isFavorite: false, } setContactsList(prevContactsList => [...prevContactsList, newContact]) setName('') setMobileNo('') } return (

## Concepts in Focus Reconciliation Virtual DOM React Batch Updating Setter Function with Callback Object Syntax Callback Syntax Children Prop Passing Text as Children Accessing Children Controlled vs Uncontrolled Input
## 1. Reconciliation ### 1.1 Virtual DOM A Virtual DOM is a JS object, which is the virtual representation of an HTML DOM. Whenever new elements are added to the UI, a virtual DOM is created. React compares new virtual DOM with current virtual DOM, and the difference will be efficiently updated to HTML DOM. So, the Virtual DOM helps to render the UI more performantly. React only updates what's necessary. This process is known as **Reconciliation**.
## 2. React Batch Updating React combines multiple `setCount()` calls into single update. **Example**: ```jsx const Counter = () => { const [count, setCount] = useState(0) console.log("Component Re-rendered") const onIncrement = () => { setCount(prevCount => prevCount + 1) setCount(prevCount => prevCount + 1) setCount(prevCount => prevCount + 1) }... return (...

Count {count}

...
...) }... ``` In the above example, the function is called only once as React combines multiple `setCount()` calls into a single update.
## 3. Setter Function with Callback ### 3.1 Value Syntax Value Syntax is used while updating the state to a value. ```jsx setCount(count+ 2) ``` ### 3.2 Callback Syntax Callback Syntax is used while updating the state to a value that is computed based on the previous state. ```jsx setCount(prevCount => prevCount+ 1 ) ``` **Example**: ```jsx const Counter = () => { const [count, setCount] = useState(0) console.log("Component Re-rendered") const onIncrement = () => { setCount(count + 1) setCount(count + 1) setCount(count + 1) }... return (...

Count {count}

...
...) }... ``` Here Value Syntax is used to update the count State Updater function is asynchronous and **count value** is same for every setCount statement when we update using value syntax Now, Lets see the same with callback syntax. ```jsx const Counter = () => { const [count, setCount] = useState(0) console.log("Component Re-rendered") const onIncrement = () => { setCount(prevCount => prevCount + 1) setCount(prevCount => prevCount + 1) setCount(prevCount => prevCount + 1) }... return (...

Count {count}

...
...) }... ``` Setter function with Callback syntax receives **prevState** as argument The callback function will receive **updated state** value when it is executed
## 4. Children Prop The **children** is a special prop that is automatically passed to every React Component. The **JSX Element/Text** included in between the opening and closing tags are considered children. ### 4.1 Passing Text as Children **File**: src/components/Post/index.jsx ```jsx import "./index.css" import SocialButton from "./SocialButton" const Post = () => (

Hooks are a new addition

Like
) export default Post ``` ### 4.2 Accessing Children **File**: src/components/SocialButton/index.jsx ```jsx import "./index.css" const SocialButton = (props) => ( ) export default SocialButton ```
## 5. Controlled vs Uncontrolled Input In React, the Input Element `value` can be handled in two ways: - Controlled Input - Uncontrolled Input ### 5.1 Controlled Input If the Input Element `value` is handled by a React State, then it is called **Controlled Input**. It is the React Suggested way to handle the Input Element value. **Example**: ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; const App = () => { const [userInput, setUserInput] = useState(""); const onChangeUserInput = (event) => { setUserInput(event.target.value); }; return ( <>

{userInput}

); }; export default App; ``` ### 5.2 Uncontrolled Input If the Input Element `value` is handled by the browser itself, then it is called **Uncontrolled Input**. **Example**: ```jsx import { useState } from "react"; const App = () => { const [userInput, setUserInput] = useState(""); const onChangeUserInput = (event) => { setUserInput(event.target.value); }; return ( <>

{userInput}

); }; export default App; ```

## Concepts in Focus Web Apps React Router Components Routing Example
## 1. Web Apps Web Apps are of two types, based on how we get content: - Multi-page application (MPA) - Single-page application (SPA) ### 1.1 Multi-page application (MPA) - Every URL is associated with corresponding resources (HTML, CSS, JS). - The browser downloads these resources when you access them or navigate between URLs. ### 1.2 Single-page application (SPA) - All URLs are associated with a single HTML page. - On navigating we only get the additional content (Component - HTML, CSS, JS). #### 1.2.1 Advantages of using Single-page application (SPA) - Faster Page loading - since they load only necessary Component (HTML, CSS, JS) resources on subsequent requests. - React is mainly used to build Single-page applications.
## 2. React Router Components In React, we build Single-page applications using **React Router**. To implement routing, React Router provides various components: - `BrowserRouter` - `Link` - `Route` - `Routes` ### 2.1 BrowserRouter To add routing wrap all the Components with `BrowserRouter`. **Syntax**: ```jsx ... ``` ### 2.2 Link `Link` Component creates hyperlinks that allows to navigate around in application. **Syntax**: ```jsx Display Text ``` The `to` prop specifies absolute path. ### 2.3 Route The `Route` component renders specific UI component when path matches current URL. ```jsx } /> ``` If user enters undefined Path, the Component won’t be rendered ### 2.3 Routes `` is a new component introduced in **React Router v6**. It is used to **wrap multiple Route components** The `` component will only render the **first route** that matches the path ```jsx } /> } /> ``` If no path matches, no component will be rendered Let see how to add "Not Found" Route ```jsx } /> } /> } /> ``` Here, We have added a new Route where the path is set as `*` This path acts as a **catch-all for all the paths** that are not matched by the previous Route components

**Mistake**:

**File**: src/App.jsx

```jsx
import {...} from 'react-router'
...
const App = () => (
  <BrowserRouter>
    <Header />
    <Routes>
      ...
      <Route path="/blogs/id" element={<BlogItemDetails/>} />
      <Route element={<NotFound/>} />
    </Routes>
  </BrowserRouter>
)
export default App
```


**Route params** are parameters whose values are **set dynamically** in the page URL

Their syntax should start with a colon(:) followed by name 

Ex:  :id, :books,  etc



**Solution**:

**File**: src/App.jsx

```jsx
import {...} from 'react-router'
...
const App = () => (
  <BrowserRouter>
    <Header />
    <Routes>
      ...
      <Route path="/blogs/:id" element={<BlogItemDetails/>} />
      <Route element={<NotFound/>} />
    </Routes>
  </BrowserRouter>
)
export default App
```

unit_title: Effect Hook and Rules of Hooks | Reading Material
content:
## Concepts in Focus Effect Hook Updating Browser title using Effect Hook Accessing State values Rules of Hooks Scoreboard Application Code
## 1. Effect Hook React provides a built-in hook called `useEffect` that allows executing logic after the component render In general this `useEffect` hook can be simply called as **Effect hook** Using Effect Hook we can perform actions like, - Making API Call - Using Scheduler methods like setInterval( ) - DOM Manipulations, etc. **Syntax**: ```jsx useEffect(effect); ``` The `useEffect` accepts **effect** as an argument, where the effect is a **callback function** ### 1.1 Updating Browser title using Effect Hook Using the **title** property provided by the DOM object we can update the Browser title Import the `useEffect` from the `react` and call it inside the component **File:** src/components/Scoreboard/index.jsx ```jsx import {useState, useEffect } from 'react' ... const Scoreboard = () => { const [score, setScore] = useState(0) useEffect(() => { document.title = `Score: ${score}` }) ... return ( ...

{score}

... ) } export default Scoreboard ``` - React keeps track of the effect and **executes** it **after** the **render** - All the state variables and props are accessible to the Effect ### 1.2 Accessing State values If you access the new state value immediately after updating the state in Event handlers, you may not get the updated value because **state updates are asynchronous** ```jsx|UGLY const onIncrement = () => { setScore(prevScore => prevScore + 1) document.title = `Score: ${score}` } ``` Use Effect Hook, if you want to access new state values immediately after updating the state ```jsx|CLEAN useEffect(() => { document.title = `Score: ${score}` }) ```
## 2. Rules of Hooks While writing/using the hooks you should follow the below rules, - **Rule 1:** Hooks should be called only at the top level - **Rule 2:** Hooks should be called only inside React Function Components and Custom Hooks React provides the **eslint-plugin-react-hooks** package that throws errors if we don’t follow these rules
## 3. Scoreboard Application Code Use the below command to get the Final Code for Scoreboard Application ```sh ccbp start RHSIVRZE65 ```

<Section id="section1">

## 1. Execution Context and Local Storage

### 1.1 Execution Context


The **environment** in which JS Code runs is called **Execution Context**

Execution context contains all the variables, objects, and functions

It is **destroyed** and **recreated** whenever we reload an Application


### 1.2 Storage Mechanisms

- **Client-Side Data Storage**
 - Storing Data on the Client (user’s machine)


- **Server-Side Data Storage**
 - Storing Data on the Server using some kind of Database


### 1.3 Local Storage

Local Storage allows web applications to store **data locally** within the **User's Browser**


It is a Storage **Object**

Data can be stored in the form of **key-value** pairs. Value provided should always be a **string**

To access and work with Local Storage, we have below methods:

- `setItem()`
- `getItem()`
- `clear()`
- `removeItem()`


**Syntax**

- **Storing an Item**

```jsx
localStorage.setItem("Key", "Value")
```


- **Getting an Item**

```jsx
localStorage.getItem("Key")
```
</Section>


<Section id = "section2">

## 2. JSON Strings

**JavaScript Object Notation (JSON)** is a data **representation format** used for:

- Storing Data (Client/Server)
- Exchanging Data between Client and Server

Number, String, Boolean, Array, Objects and Null are the supported types of JSON.


### 2.1 JSON Methods

JavaScript provides JSON methods to convert Data into JSON format


**JSON.stringify()**:

It converts the given value into **JSON String**

It **store values** in the form of **strings** 

**Syntax**

```jsx
JSON.stringify( value )
```


**JSON.parse()**:

It parses a JSON String and returns a JS Object

After getting the values, this method convert them into **corresponding data types**


**Syntax**

```jsx
JSON.parse( string )
```

</Section>

<Section id = "section3">

## 3. Persisting data while using React Hooks

### 3.1 Storing values in Local Storage

In `useEffect` we have access to the updated state values, so we write the logic for storing values inside it.

**File:** src/components/BmiCalculator/index.jsx

```jsx
...
const BmiCalculator = () => {
  const [height, setHeight] = useState(170)
  const [weight, setWeight] = useState(60)

  useEffect(() => {
    document.title = `Your BMI: ${getBmi(weight, height)}`
    localStorage.setItem("height", JSON.stringify(height))
    localStorage.setItem("weight", JSON.stringify(weight))
  })
  ...
  return (...)
}

export default BmiCalculator
```

### 3.2 Getting values from the Local Storage

Once after getting the values from the Local Storage, provide them as the initial values for the `useState` hook

**File:** src/components/BmiCalculator/index.jsx

```jsx
...
const BmiCalculator = () => {
  const storedHeight = JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem("height"))
  const storedWeight = JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem("weight"))

  const [height, setHeight] = useState(storedHeight !== null ? storedHeight : 170)
  const [weight, setWeight] = useState(storedWeight !== null ? storedWeight : 60)

  useEffect(() => {
    ...
    localStorage.setItem("height", JSON.stringify(height))
    localStorage.setItem("weight", JSON.stringify(weight))
  })
  ...
}

export default BmiCalculator
```

</Section>

<Section id = "section4">

## 4. Tips for using the Effect Hook

### 4.1 Using Multiple useEffects to Separate Concerns

Just like useState, you can also use multiple useEffects to **separate unrelated logic** into different effects based on what it is doing

**File:** src/components/BmiCalculator/index.jsx

```jsx
...
const BmiCalculator = () => {
  ...
  useEffect(() => {
    document.title = `Your BMI: ${getBmi(weight, height)}`
  })

  useEffect(() => {
    localStorage.setItem("weight", JSON.stringify(weight))
  })

  useEffect(() => {
    localStorage.setItem("height", JSON.stringify(height))
  })
  ...
}

export default BmiCalculator
```

### 4.2 Optimizing Performance by Skipping Effects

In some cases, executing the effect after every render creates a performance problem, It can be optimized by **Skipping** the **effect** when it is not required

The `useEffect` accepts another argument called **Dependency Array**, using which we can control the execution of effect

**Syntax**:

```jsx
useEffect(effect, [var1, var2, ...])
```

- The values we pass to the dependency array are called dependencies, which can be state variables or props
- Effect will be executed whenever the dependencies are changed

#### Without Dependencies

The Effect executes after every render

```jsx
useEffect(effect);
```

#### Passing Empty Dependency Array

The Effect executes only once after the first render

```jsx
useEffect(effect, []);
```

#### Passing Dependencies

The Effect executes when dependencies are changed

```jsx
useEffect(effect, [var1, var2,...])
```

**File:** src/components/BmiCalculator/index.jsx

```jsx
...
const BmiCalculator = () => {
  ...
  useEffect(() => {
     document.title = `Your BMI: ${getBmi(weight, height)}`
  }, [height, weight])

  useEffect(() => {
    localStorage.setItem("height", JSON.stringify(height))
  }, [height])

  useEffect(() => {
   localStorage.setItem("weight", JSON.stringify(weight))
  }, [weight])
  ...
}

export default BmiCalculator
```

</Section>

<Section id="section5">

## 5. BmiCalculator Application Code

Use the below command to get the Final Code for BmiCalculator Application

```sh
ccbp start RHSIVTWEU5
```

## Concepts in Focus Making API Call while using React Hooks API Make API Call using fetch() Effects should be Synchronous Writing Asynchronous Function Set Leaderboard Data Dependency Array Displaying Loader
## 1. Making API Call while using React Hooks Lets understand this by building a simple Leaderboard Application #### What to Achieve? - Make an **API Call** and get the **Leaderboard data** - Display different views in our Application such as: Loading View and Success View Lets begin with some initial code. HERE COMES THE INITIAL CODE Let's make an API Call to get the Leaderboard data ### 1.1 API **HTTP Request** - Method: GET - URL: https://apis2.ccbp.in/leaderboard-v2 **HTTP Response** - Body: List of Users and their details In general, it’s a best practice to **make** an **API Call after the render** So that it doesn’t block rendering the component ### 1.2 Make API Call using fetch() In React, **useEffect** allows executing logic after the **component render** In Function Component, we can use `Effect Hook` to make an API Call after the component render API Call using the `fetch()` method works asynchronously, so we need to write the API Call logic in an **async function** ### 1.3 Effects should be Synchronous It's not a good practice to make an effect asynchronous because it may lead to unexpected results ```jsx|UGLY useEffect(async() => { await fetch(url, options) ... }) ``` If you need an asynchronous function, define and call the async function inside the Effect ```jsx|CLEAN useEffect(() => { const funName = async() => { await fetch(url, options) ... } funName() }) ``` ### 1.4 Writing Asynchronous Function The following is the final Asynchronous function to fetch the data. **File**: src/components/Leaderboard/index.jsx ```jsx const Leaderboard = () => { useEffect(() => { const getLeaderboardData = async () => { const url = "https://apis2.ccbp.in/leaderboard-v2" const response = await fetch(url, options) const responseData = await response.json() console.log(responseData) } getLeaderboardData() }) ... }... ``` But usually, In the front-end we use **camelCase** for naming conventions So, we need to convert **snake_case to camelCase** ```jsx ... const getLeaderboardData = async () => {... const data = await response.json() const formattedData = responseData.leaderboard_data.map(eachUser => ({ id: eachUser.id, rank: eachUser.rank, name: eachUser.name, profileImgUrl: eachUser.profile_image_url, score: eachUser.score, language: eachUser.language, timeSpent: eachUser.time_spent, })) console.log(updatedData) } ... ```
## 2. Set Leaderboard Data First Lets initalise and update state. **File**: src/components/Leaderboard/index.jsx ```jsx import {useState} from 'react' const Leaderboard = () => { const [leaderboardData, setLeaderboardData] = useState([]) useEffect(() => { const getLeaderboardData = async () => { ... const response = await fetch(url) const formattedData = responseData.leaderboard_data.map(...) setLeaderboardData(formattedData) } getLeaderboardData() }) ... }... ``` Now, Lets render the Leaderboard **File**: src/components/Leaderboard/index.jsx ```jsx import {useState} from 'react' const Leaderboard = () => { const [leaderboardData, setLeaderboardData] = useState([]) const renderLeaderboard = () => (
    {renderLeaderboardHeader()} {leaderboardData.map(eachUser => ( ))}
) useEffect(() => { const getLeaderboardData = async () => { ... const response = await fetch(url) const formattedData = responseData.leaderboard_data.map(...) setLeaderboardData(formattedData) } getLeaderboardData() }) ... return (
{renderLeaderboard()}
) ... }... ``` Now, If you check in the console, you can observe that the API is getting called continously. When updating state in React using arrays or objects, a **new reference is created** each time the state setter function is called So, **React will trigger re-render**, even if the internal content looks identical
## 3. Dependency Array We can **control** the **execution** of **Effect** by passing **Dependency Array** ```jsx useEffect(effect) ``` The Effect executes after every render ```jsx useEffect(effect, [var1, var2,...]) ``` This Effect executes when dependencies are changed ```jsx useEffect(effect, []) ``` This Effect executes **only once** after the first render So, In our scenario also, Effect should execute only once, So use the last one It’s always a best practice to pass a **Dependency Array** while using the **Effect Hook**
## 4. Displaying Loader Search for `react-spinners` package in npm This command **installs** the **react-spinners package** which can be used all over the application ```bash npm install react-spinners --save ``` Now, Lets import and use Loader Component **File**: src/components/Leaderboard/index.jsx ```jsx import BeatLoader from 'react-spinners/BeatLoader' const Leaderboard = () => { const [leaderboardData, setLeaderboardData] = useState([]) useEffect(() => {...}, []) const renderLoader = () => (
) const renderLeaderboard = () => (...) return (
{renderLeaderboard()}
) }... ``` Now initialise state and show loader Make sure to set loading state as false after the API call ```jsx import BeatLoader from 'react-spinners/BeatLoader' const Leaderboard = () => { const [leaderboardData, setLeaderboardData] = useState([]) const [isLoading, setIsLoading] = useState(true) useEffect(() => { const getLeaderboardData = async () => { ... const formattedData = responseData.leaderboard_data.map(...) setLeaderboardData(formattedData) setIsLoading(false) } getLeaderboardData() }, []) const renderLoader = () => (
) const renderLeaderboard = () => (...) return (
{isLoading ? renderLoader() : renderLeaderboard()}
) }... ```

## Concepts in Focus Component Life Cycle Showing and Hiding the Clock Displaying the Current time Updating the time for every second Avoiding unnecessary scheduling of intervals Clearing the Intervals Cleanup function Adding Cleanup function
## 1. Component Life Cycle Every React Component goes through **three phases** throughout its lifetime - **Mounting Phase** - Here, The instance of a component is **created** and **inserted** into the DOM - **Updating Phase** - A component is updated whenever there is a **change** in the **component's state** - **Unmounting Phase** - Component instance is **removed** from the DOM Let’s understand with a simple Clock Application #### What to Achieve? **Display** the **current time** using Scheduler methods & handle mounting/unmounting cases Lets begin with the initial codes in IDE.
## 2. Showing and Hiding the Clock Let’s show and hide Clock based on button click Initialise State and based on the state value, we are **conditionally rendering** the **Button text** **Logical && Operator** can be handy for conditionally including a component **File**: src/App.jsx ```jsx ... const App = () => { const [isMounted, setIsMounted] = useState(true) const onToggleClock = () => { setIsMounted(prevIsMounted => !prevIsMounted) } return (
{isMounted && }
) } export default App ```
## 3. Displaying the Current time We use `Date` Built-in Constructor function to display the current time **Syntax** ```jsx const date = new Date() console.log(date.toLocaleTimeString()) ``` **File**: src/components/Clock/index.jsx ```jsx import { useState } from 'react' ... const Clock = () => { const [date, setDate] = useState(new Date()) return (

Clock

{date.toLocaleTimeString()}

) } export default Clock ```
## 4. Updating the time for every second We use **Scheduler methods** for this. | Method | Purpose | |--------------|-------------------------------------------------------------| | `setInterval()` | Executes callback function repeatedly at a specified interval | | `clearInterval()` | Clears the interval set by `setInterval()` | | `setTimeout()` | Executes callback function only once after specified time | | `clearTimeout()` | Clears the timeout set by `setTimeout()` | Here, the Component with current time is already rendered Let’s use `setInterval()` to update time every second React provides a built-in hook called `useEffect` that allows **executing** logic after the **component render** We schedule an interval inside the `useEffect` for updating time after the component render **File:** src/components/Clock/index.js ```jsx import { useState, useEffect } from 'react' ... const Clock = () => { const [date, setDate] = useState(new Date()) useEffect(() => { const timerId = setInterval(() => { setDate(new Date()) }, 1000) }) return (
...
) } export default Clock ``` ### 4.1 Avoiding unnecessary scheduling of intervals In our code, we can observe that Effect executes multiple times, and many intervals are scheduled, but one Interval is enough to update the time for every second The effect should execute and an interval should be scheduled only once, so pass **empty dependency array** as a second argument to the useEffect **File:** src/components/Clock/index.js ```jsx import { useState, useEffect } from 'react' ... const Clock = () => { const [date, setDate] = useState(new Date()) useEffect(() => { const timerId = setInterval(() => { setDate(new Date()) }, 1000) },[]) return (
...
) } export default Clock ```
## 5. Clearing the Intervals Once an Interval is scheduled, the `setInterval` **executes** the **callback function** repeatedly **until we stop it** Even after the Component **unmounting**, **setInterval** continues to **update** the **state** So, we use `clearInterval()` method to do it ### 5.1 Cleanup function The effect which we pass to the `useEffect` can return a function called Cleanup function **Syntax**: ```jsx useEffect(() => { // function body return () => { // cleanup } } ) ``` In the Cleanup function we can perform actions like Clearing timers, Cancelling Network calls, etc The Cleanup function will execute - When the component unmounts - Before the execution of next effect ### 5.2 Adding Cleanup function **File:** src/components/Clock/index.js ```jsx ... ... const Clock = () => { const [date, setDate] = useState(new Date()) useEffect(() => { const timerId = setInterval(() => { setDate(new Date()) }, 1000) return () => { clearInterval(timerId) console.log('Cleanup Function executed') } }, []) return (
...
) } ... ```

## Concepts in Focus JWT Token Storage Mechanisms Cookies Why Cookies? Cookies vs Local Storage Third Party Package Navigate Component Quick Commerce Application
## 1. JWT Token JSON Web Token is a standard used to create Access Tokens. These access tokens are also called JWT Tokens. The client uses these access tokens on every subsequent request to communicate with the Server. While making HTTP Request, we have to send an access token in the HTTP Headers with the key _Authorization_. **Example**: `Authorization: Bearer jwt_token` ### 1.1 Storing JWT Token in State When we store the JWT Token in the state, - On page refresh, the JWT token won't be available - It is difficult to pass state information to every component
## 2. Storage Mechanisms - Client-Side Data Storage - Storing Data on the Client - Server-Side Data Storage - Storing Data on the Server using some kind of Database Different types of Client-Side data storage mechanisms are: - Local Storage - **Cookies** - Session Storage - IndexedDB, etc.
## 3. Cookies A cookie is a piece of data that is stored on the user's computer by the web browser. A cookie is made up of: - Name & Value - Expires − The date the cookie will expire. If this is blank, the cookie will expire when the visitor quits the browser. - Domain − The domain name of your site. - Path − The path to the directory or web page that set the cookie. This may be blank if you want to retrieve the cookie from any directory or page. - Secure − If this field contains the word "secure", then the cookie may only be retrieved with a secure server. If this field is blank, no such restriction exists, etc. ### 3.1 Why Cookies? With cookies, we can set the expiry duration. Examples: - Banking Applications - Cookies get expired in minutes - Facebook - Cookies get expired in months or years ### 3.2 Cookies vs Local Storage | Cookies | Local Storage | | ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------ | | We can set an expiration for Cookies | Local storage data never expires | | Cookies can store up to 4KB of data | Local Storage can store up to 5 to 10 MB of data | ### 3.3 Third Party Package - js-cookie JavaScript can read, create, modify, and delete the cookies. NPM contains a **js-cookie**, a third-party package to manipulate cookies easily. **Installation Command**: ```Terminal npm install js-cookie --save ``` js-cookie methods are: - `Cookies.set()`- It is used to set the cookie - `Cookies.get()`- It is used to get the cookie - `Cookies.remove()`- It is used to remove the cookie #### 3.3.1 `Cookies.set()` **Syntax:** ```jsx Cookies.set('CookieName', 'CookieValue', {expires: DAYS}); ``` **Example:** ```jsx Cookies.set('ACCESS_TOKEN', 'Us1L90PXl...', {expires: 1}); ``` #### 3.3.2 `Cookies.get()` It returns `undefined` if the cookie expires or does not exist. **Syntax:** ```jsx Cookies.get('CookieName'); ``` **Example:** ```jsx Cookies.get('ACCESS_TOKEN'); ``` #### 3.3.3 `Cookies.remove()` **Syntax:** ```jsx Cookies.remove('CookieName'); ``` **Example:** ```jsx Cookies.remove('ACCESS_TOKEN'); ```
## 4. Navigate Component React Router provides `` component which allows us to **navigate between Routes** **Syntax:** ```jsx ``` **Example:** ```jsx ``` ### 4.1 Navigate Component vs navigate() - Use **Navigate Component**, when you have to stop displaying UI and navigate to a route - In all other cases, `use navigate()` Ex: `onSubmit`, `onClick` event callback functions

## Concepts in Focus Using Navigate Component in Callbacks or Event Listeners Using useNavigate() in function body/return, instead of Navigate Component When request object keys are incorrect Not Updating the State when required
## 1. Using Navigate Component in Callbacks or Event Listeners **Mistake**: As the `Navigate` Component returns JSX, it should not be used in event handlers. **File**: src/components/Header/index.jsx ```jsx import {Link, Navigate} from 'react-router' ... const Header = () => { const onClickLogout = () => { Cookies.remove('jwt_token') return } return (...) } export default Header ``` **Explanation**: - `Navigate` Component returns JSX and it is used to render the UI in the specific Route. - To **navigate** to the specific Routes in **event handlers** or **callbacks** used `useNavigate()` hook **Solution**: **File**: src/components/Header/index.jsx ```jsx import {Link, useNavigate} from 'react-router' ... const Header = props => { const navigate = useNavigate() const onClickLogout = () => { Cookies.remove('jwt_token') navigate('/login', {replace: true}) } return (...) } export default Header ```
## 2. Using useNavigate() in function body/return, instead of Navigate Component **Mistake**: `navigate()` is used in event handlers or callbacks to navigate to specific Routes **File**: src/components/LoginForm/index.jsx ```jsx ... const LoginForm = () => { ... if (Cookies.get('jwt_token') !== undefined) { return navigate('/') } return (
...
} export default LoginForm ``` **Explanation**: In Rendering, **JSX** is displayed and **Navigate Component** returns JSX. So it’s a best practice to use **Navigate Component** while navigating in **rendering** **Solution**: **File**: src/components/LoginForm/index.jsx ```jsx import {Navigate} from 'react-router' ... const LoginForm = () => { ... if (jwtToken !== undefined) { return } return (...) } export default LoginForm ```
## 3. When request object keys are incorrect **Mistake**: For example below in the request object, we need to send `username` and `password` but `userName` is sent instead of the `username`. **File**: src/components/LoginForm/index.jsx ```jsx const LoginForm = () => { const [username, setUsername] = useState('') const submitForm = async event => { event.preventDefault() const userDetails = {userName: username, password: password} const url = 'https://apis.ccbp.in/login' const options = { method: 'POST', ... } const response = await fetch(url, options) ... }... return (...) } ``` **Solution**: **File**: src/components/LoginForm/index.jsx ```jsx const LoginForm = () => { const [username, setUsername] = useState('') const submitForm = async event => { event.preventDefault() const userDetails = {username: username, password: password} const url = 'https://apis.ccbp.in/login' const options = { method: 'POST', ... } const response = await fetch(url, options) ... }... return (...) } ```
## 4. Not Updating the State when required **Mistake**: On login failure, the `showSubmitError` state should be updated to display the error message **File**: src/components/LoginForm/index.jsx ```jsx const LoginForm = () => { const [showSubmitError, setShowSubmitError] = useState(false) const [errorMsg, setErrorMsg] = useState('') ... const onSubmitFailure = errorMsg => { setErrorMsg(errorMsg) } return (... {showSubmitError &&

*{errorMsg}

} ... )... } ``` **Solution**: **File**: src/components/LoginForm/index.jsx ```jsx const LoginForm = () => { const [showSubmitError, setShowSubmitError] = useState(false) const [errorMsg, setErrorMsg] = useState('') ... const onSubmitFailure = errorMsg => { setShowSubmitError(true) setErrorMsg(errorMsg) } return (... {showSubmitError &&

*{errorMsg}

} ... )... } ```

## Concepts in Focus Get Exclusive Prime Deals Integrating APIs Get Exclusive Prime Deals API Call Possible Views Success View Failure View Loading View Quick Commerce Application Best Practices State Variable-isLoading State Variable-apiStatus
##1. Integrating APIs ###1.1 Get Exclusive Prime Deals - **Exclusive Prime deals** are for **Prime Users**. - **All Products** are for both **Prime** and **Non-Prime users**.
##2. API Call Possible Views ### 2.1 Success View When the `Prime User` is logged in and accessed `Prime Deals` Section then we should show the `Exclusive Prime Deals` section. ### 2.2 Failure View When the `Non-prime User` is logged in and accessed `Prime Deals` Section then we should show the `Get Exclusive Deals` section. #### Reasons for API Call Failure : - Sending UnAuthorized User credentials - Not specifying Authorization header - Using the wrong HTTP method ### 2.3 Loading View When the `Prime` or `Non-prime User` is logged in and accessed `Prime Deals` Section, we should show the `Loading view` until data is in progress.
