Introduction to Node JS | Reading Material
content:
<Section>

## Concepts in Focus

<IndexList>
    <IndexItem href="1-mern-stack">MERN Stack</IndexItem>
    <IndexItem href="2-node-js">Node JS</IndexItem>
    <IndexItem href="3-running-javascript-using-node-js">Running JavaScript Using Node JS</IndexItem>
    <IndexList>
        <IndexItem href="31-node-repl-read-eval-print-loop">Node REPL (Read-Eval-Print-Loop)</IndexItem>
        <IndexItem href="32-node-cli">Node CLI</IndexItem>
    </IndexList>
    <IndexItem href="4-module">Module</IndexItem>
    <IndexList>
        <IndexItem href="41-common-js-module-exports">Common JS Module Exports</IndexItem>
        <IndexItem href="42-modern-js-module-exports">Modern JS Module Exports</IndexItem>
    </IndexList>
</IndexList>

</Section>

<Section>

## 1. MERN Stack

MERN stands for **MongoDB**, **Express JS**, **React JS** and **Node JS**.

It is a **JavaScript Stack** that is used for easier & faster deployment of full-stack web applications.

</Section>

<Section>

## 2. Node JS

Node JS is a JavaScript environment that executes JavaScript code outside a web browser.

**Why Node JS?**

- Cross-Platform (Windows, Linux, Mac OS X, etc.)
- Huge number of third-party packages
- Open Source
- Massive Community

</Section>

<Section>

## 3. Running JavaScript Using Node JS

We can run JavaScript using Node JS in 2 ways.

- Node REPL (Similar to browser console)
- Node CLI

### 3.1 Node REPL (Read-Eval-Print-Loop)

Type `node` in the terminal and press `Enter`.

```Terminal
root@123# node
Welcome to Node.js v12.18.3.
Type ".help" for more information.
> const a = 1
undefined
> const b = 2
undefined
> a+b
3
>
```

Type `.exit` and press `Enter` to exit from the Node REPL.

```Terminal
root@123# node
Welcome to Node.js v12.18.3.
Type ".help" for more information.
> const a = 1
undefined
> const b = 2
undefined
> a+b
3
> .exit
root@123:/home/workspace#
```

### 3.2 Node CLI

We can write JavaScript to a file and can run using Node CLI.

```js
//index.js

const greetings = (name) => {
  console.log(`Hello ${name}`);
};

greetings("Raju");
greetings("Abhi");
```

```terminal
root@123# node index.js
Hello Raju
Hello Abhi
```

<MultiLineNote>Save the file whenever the code changes.</MultiLineNote>

</Section>

<Section>

## 4. Module

In Node JS, each JavaScript file is treated as a separate module. These are known as the **Common JS/Node JS** Modules.

To access one module from another module, we have Module Exports.

- Common JS Module Exports
  - Default Exports
  - Named Exports
- Modern JS Module Exports
  - Default Exports
  - Named Exports

### 4.1 Common JS Module Exports

### 4.1.1 Default Exports

#### Exporting Module

The `module.exports` is a special object included in every JavaScript file in the Node JS application by default.

```js
//calculator.js

const add = (a, b) => {
  return a + b;
};
module.exports = add;
```

#### Importing Module

To import a module which is the local file, use the **require()** function with the **relative path** of the module (file name).

```js
//index.js

const add = require("./calculator");

console.log(add(6, 3));
```

Output

```terminal
root@123# node index.js
9
```

### 4.1.2 Named Exports

We can have multiple named exports per module.

#### Exporting Module

```js
//calculator.js

const add = (a, b) => {
  return a + b;
};
const sub = (a, b) => {
  return a - b;
};

exports.add = add;
exports.sub = sub;
```

#### Importing Module

```js
//index.js

const { add, sub } = require("./calculator");

console.log(add(6, 3));

console.log(sub(6, 3));
```

Output

```console
root@123# node index.js
9
3
```

### 4.2 Modern JS Module Exports

Modern JS Modules are known as **ES6 Modules**.

The **export** and **import** keywords are introduced for exporting and importing one or more members in a module.

### 4.2.1 Default Exports

#### Exporting Module

```js
//calculator.mjs

const add = (a, b) => {
  return a + b;
};
export default add;
```

#### Importing Module

```js
//index.mjs

import add from "./calculator.mjs";
console.log(add(6, 3));
```

Output

```console
root@123# node index.mjs
9
```

### 4.2.2 Named Exports

#### Exporting Module

```js
//calculator.mjs

export const add = (a, b) => {
  return a + b;
};
export const sub = (a, b) => {
  return a - b;
};
```

#### Importing Module

```js
//index.mjs

import { add, sub } from "./calculator.mjs";

console.log(add(6, 3));
console.log(sub(6, 3));
```

Output

```console
root@123# node index.mjs
9
3
```

<MultiLineNote>
- We need to specify `.mjs` extension while importing ES6 Modules.
- We may or may not need to specify `.js` while importing Common JS Modules.
</MultiLineNote>

</Section>

<Section>

## Concepts in Focus

<IndexList>
    <IndexItem href="1-default-exports">Default Exports</IndexItem>
    <IndexList>
        <IndexItem href="11-exporting-a-variable-while-defining">Exporting a variable while defining</IndexItem>
        <IndexItem href="12-exporting-a-variable-after-defining">Exporting a variable after defining</IndexItem>
        <IndexItem href="13-exporting-a-value-or-an-expression">Exporting a value or an expression</IndexItem>
        <IndexItem href="14-exporting-a-function-while-defining">Exporting a function while defining</IndexItem>
        <IndexItem href="15-exporting-a-function-after-defining">Exporting a function after defining</IndexItem>
        <IndexItem href="16-exporting-a-class-while-defining">Exporting a class while defining</IndexItem>
        <IndexItem href="17-exporting-a-class-after-defining">Exporting a class after defining</IndexItem>
    </IndexList>
    <IndexItem href="2-named-exports">Named Exports</IndexItem>
    <IndexList>
        <IndexItem href="21-exporting-multiple-variables-while-defining">Exporting multiple variables while defining</IndexItem>
        <IndexItem href="22-exporting-multiple-variables-after-defining">Exporting multiple variables after defining</IndexItem>
        <IndexItem href="23-exporting-multiple-values-and-expressions">Exporting multiple values and expressions</IndexItem>
        <IndexItem href="24-exporting-multiple-functions-while-defining">Exporting multiple functions while defining</IndexItem>
        <IndexItem href="25-exporting-multiple-functions-after-defining">Exporting multiple functions after defining</IndexItem>
        <IndexItem href="26-exporting-multiple-classes-while-defining">Exporting multiple classes while defining</IndexItem>
        <IndexItem href="27-exporting-multiple-classes-after-defining">Exporting multiple classes after defining</IndexItem>
    </IndexList>
</IndexList>

</Section>

<Section>

Let's see different scenarios that we may come across while exporting.

## 1. Default Exports

With Default Exports, we can import modules with any name.

### 1.1 Exporting a variable while defining

We cannot export boolean, number, string, null, undefined, objects, and arrays while defining.

**Example**:

```js
//sample.js

module.exports = let value = 5;
```

```js
//index.js

const num = require("./sample.js");
```

Output

```terminal
root@123# node index.js
/index.js:3
module.exports = let value = 5;
                     ^

SyntaxError: Unexpected identifier
    at wrapSafe (internal/modules/cjs/loader.js:1053:16)
    ...
```

### 1.2 Exporting a variable after defining

We can export boolean, number, string, null, undefined, objects, and arrays after defining.

**Example**:

```js
//sample.js

let value = 5;
module.exports = value;
```

```js
//index.js

const value = require("./sample.js");

console.log(value);
```

Output

```terminal
root@123# node index.js
5
```

### 1.3 Exporting a value or an expression

We can export a value or an expression directly.

**Example**:

```js
//sample.js

module.exports = 5 * 3;
```

```js
//index.js

const result = require("./sample.js");

console.log(result);
```

Output

```terminal
root@123# node index.js
15
```

### 1.4 Exporting a function while defining

We can export a function while defining.

**Example**:

```js
//sample.js

module.exports = function (num1, num2) {
  return num1 + num2;
};
```

```js
//index.js

const sum = require("./sample.js");

console.log(sum(2, 6));
```

Output

```terminal
root@123# node index.js
8
```

### 1.5 Exporting a function after defining

We can export a function after defining.

**Example**:

```js
//sample.js

function sum(num1, num2) {
  return num1 + num2;
}
module.exports = sum;
```

```js
//index.js

const sum = require("./sample.js");

console.log(sum(2, 6));
```

Output

```terminal
root@123# node index.js
8
```

### 1.6 Exporting a class while defining

We can export a class while defining.

**Example**:

```js
//sample.js

module.exports = class StudentDetails {
  constructor(name, age) {
    this.name = name;
    this.age = age;
  }
};
```

```js
//index.js

const StudentDetails = require("./sample.js");

const studentDetails = new StudentDetails("Ram", 15);

console.log(studentDetails);

console.log(studentDetails.name);
```

Output

```terminal
root@123# node index.js
StudentDetails { name: 'Ram', age: 15 }
Ram
```

### 1.7 Exporting a class after defining

We can export a class after defining.

**Example**:

```js
//sample.js

class StudentDetails {
  constructor(name, age) {
    this.name = name;
    this.age = age;
  }
}
module.exports = StudentDetails;
```

```js
//index.js

const StudentDetails = require("./sample.js");

const studentDetails = new StudentDetails("Ram", 15);

console.log(studentDetails);

console.log(studentDetails.name);
```

Output

```terminal
root@123# node index.js
```

<Section>

## 2. Named Exports

### 2.1 Exporting multiple variables while defining

We cannot export boolean, number, string, null, undefined, objects, and arrays while defining.

**Example**:

```js
//sample.js

exports.value = let value = 5;
exports.studentName = let studentName = "Rahul";
```

```js
//index.js

const { value, studentName } = require("./sample");

console.log(value);

console.log(studentName);
```

```terminal
exports.value = let value = 5;
                    ^^^^^

SyntaxError: Unexpected identifier
    at wrapSafe (internal/modules/cjs/loader.js:1053:16)
```

### 2.2 Exporting multiple variables after defining

We can export multiple variables after defining.

**Example**:

```js
//sample.js

exports.value = value;
let studentName = "Rahul";
exports.studentName = studentName;
```

```js
//index.js

console.log(value);

console.log(studentName);
```

Output

```terminal
root@123# node index.js

Rahul
```

### 2.3 Exporting multiple values and expressions

We can export multiple values and expressions.

**Example**:

```js

let value = 2;
exports.sum = 2 + 3;
exports.sub = 3 - value;
```

```js
//index.js

const { sum, sub } = require("./sample");

console.log(sum);

console.log(sub);
```

Output

```terminal
root@123# node index.js
5
1
```

### 2.4 Exporting multiple functions while defining

We can export multiple functions while defining.

**Example**:

```js
//sample.js

exports.sum = function (num1, num2) {
  return num1 + num2;
};

exports.sub = function sub(num1, num2) {
  return num1 - num2;
};
```

```js
//index.js

const { sum, sub } = require("./sample");

console.log(sum(2, 6));

console.log(sub(8, 3));
```

Output

```terminal
root@123# node index.js
8
5
```

### 2.5 Exporting multiple functions after defining

We can export multiple functions after defining.

**Example**:

```js
//sample.js

function sum(num1, num2) {
  return num1 + num2;
}

exports.sum = sum;

function sub(num1, num2) {
  return num1 - num2;
}

exports.sub = sub;
```

```js
//index.js

const { sum, sub } = require("./sample");

console.log(sum(2, 6));

console.log(sub(8, 3));
```

Output

```terminal
root@123# node index.js
8
5
```

### 2.6 Exporting multiple classes while defining

We can export multiple classes while defining.

**Example**:

```js
//sample.js

exports.studentDetails = class StudentDetails {
  constructor(name, age) {
    this.name = name;
    this.age = age;
  }
};

exports.carDetails = class CarDetails {
  constructor(name, age) {
    this.name = name;
    this.speed = age;
  }
};
```

```js
//index.js

const { studentDetails, carDetails } = require("./sample.js");

const newStudentDetails = new studentDetails("Ram", 15);
console.log(newStudentDetails);
console.log(newStudentDetails.name);

const newCarDetails = new carDetails("Alto", "60kmph");
console.log(newCarDetails);
console.log(newCarDetails.name);
```

Output

```terminal
root@123# node index.js
StudentDetails { name: 'Ram', age: 15 }
Ram
CarDetails { name: 'Alto', speed: '60kmph' }
Alto
```

### 2.7 Exporting multiple classes after defining

We can export multiple classes after defining.

**Example**:

```js
class StudentDetails {
```

        <IndexItem href="41-date-fns">date-fns</IndexItem>

</IndexList>

<Section>

## 1. Core Modules

The Core Modules are inbuilt in Node JS.

Some of the most commonly used are:

| Module | Description             |
| ------ | ----------------------- |
| path   | Handles file paths      |
| fs     | Handles the file system |
| url    | Parses the URL strings  |

### 1.1 Path

The path module provides utilities for working with file and directory paths. It can be accessed using:

```js
const path = require("path");
```

**Example**:

```js
//index.js

const path = require("path");
const filePath = path.join("users", "ravi", "notes.txt");

console.log(filePath);
```

Output

```console
root@123# node index.js
users/ravi/notes.txt

```

<MultiLineNote>

Many developers prefer Common JS Modules to ES6 syntax as ES6 syntax is in the experimental phase.

</MultiLineNote>

</Section>

<Section>

## 2. Package

A package is a directory with one or more modules grouped.

### 2.1 Node Package Manager (NPM)

NPM is the package manager for the Node JS packages with more than one million packages.

It provides a command line tool that allows you to publish, discover, install, and develop node programs.

#### 2.1.1 CLI

NPM CLI sets up the Node JS Project to organize various modules and work with third-party packages.

| Command                             | Description                                           |
| ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| `npm init -y`                       | Initializes a project and creates a package.json file |
| `npm install <package-name> --save` | Installs the third-party packages                     |

</Section>

<Section>

## 3. Steps to create a Node JS Project

Run the below commands in the terminal.

1. Create a new directory/folder.

`mkdir myapp`

2. Move into the created folder.

`cd myapp`

3. Initialize the project.

`npm init -y`

</Section>

<Section>

## 4. Third-Party Packages

The Third-Party Packages are the external Node JS Packages.

They are developed by Node JS developers and are made available through the Node ecosystem.

### 4.1 date-fns

It is a third-party package for manipulating JavaScript dates in a browser & Node.js.

**Installation Command:**

```Terminal
npm install date-fns --save
```

#### 4.1.1 addDays

It adds the specified number of days to the given date.

**Example**:

```js
//index.js

const {addDays} = require('date-fns');
const result = addDays(new Date(2021, 0, 11), 10);

console.log(result);

```

Output

```console
root@123# node index.js
2021-01-21T00:00:00.000Z
```

<MultiLineNote>

While creating the `Date()` object, we have to provide the month index from (0-11), whereas we will get the output considering Jan=1 and Dec=12.

</MultiLineNote>

</Section>

content:
## Concepts in Focus HTTP Server Server-side Web Frameworks Express JS Network Call using Express JS Handling HTTP Request Testing Network calls Network Call to get a Today’s Date Network Call to get HTML content as an HTTP Response Sending file as an HTTP Response
## 1. HTTP Server - Works with the HTTP requests and responses - Handles the different paths - Handles the query parameters - Sends the content as HTML, CSS, etc. as an HTTP response - Works with the databases ### 1.1 Server-side Web Frameworks The Server-side Web Frameworks take care of all the above requirements. Some of the Web Frameworks are: - **Express (Node JS)** - Django (Python) - Ruby on Rails (Ruby) - Spring Boot (Java)
## 2. Express JS It is a free and open-source Server-side Web Application Framework for Node JS. It provides a robust set of features to build web and mobile applications quickly and easily. **Installation Command:** ```Terminal npm install express --save ```
## 3. Network call using Express JS 1. Creating Express server instance ```js const express = require("express"); const app = express(); ``` 2. Assigning a port number ```js app.listen(3000); ``` The app starts a server and listens on port `3000` for connections. ### 3.1 Handling HTTP Request **Syntax:** `app.METHOD(PATH, HANDLER)` - **METHOD** is an HTTP request method, in lowercase like `get`, `post`, `put`, and `delete`. - **PATH** is a path on the server. - **HANDLER** is the function executed when the PATH is matched with the requested path. #### 3.1.1 GET Request ```js const express = require("express"); const app = express(); app.get("/", (request, response) => { response.send("Hello World!"); }); app.listen(3000); ``` Whenever the code changes, we need to restart the server to reflect the changes we made.
## 4. Testing Network Calls We can test the Network calls in two ways. 1. Browser Network Tab. ![http-get-response](https://assets.ccbp.in/frontend/content/node-js/cs-3-http-get-response-img.png) ![browser-network](https://assets.ccbp.in/frontend/content/node-js/cs-3-browser-network-tab-img.png)
1. Clicking the `Send Request` in the `app.http` file. ![app-http](https://assets.ccbp.in/frontend/content/node-js/cs-3-api-testing-img.png)
## 5. Network Call to get a Today’s Date ```js const express = require("express"); const app = express(); app.get("/date", (request, response) => { let date = new Date(); response.send(`Today's date is ${date}`); }); app.listen(3000); ``` ![get-todays-date-response](https://assets.ccbp.in/frontend/content/node-js/cs-3-get-todays-date-response-img.png)
## 6. Network Call to get HTML content as an HTTP Response ### 6.1 Sending file as an HTTP Response **Syntax:** `response.sendFile(PATH, {root: __dirname });` - **PATH** is a path to the file which we want to send. - **\_\_dirname** is a variable in Common JS Modules that returns the path of the folder where the current JavaScript file is present. ```js const express = require("express"); const app = express(); app.get("/page", (request, response) => { response.sendFile("./page.html", { root: __dirname }); }); app.listen(3000); ```

<Section>

## 1. Application Programming Interface (API)

An API is a software intermediary that allows two applications to talk to each other.

For example, OLA, and UBER use Google Maps API to provide their services.

All the Network calls that we added are also the APIs.

</Section>

<Section>

## 2. Database

Express apps can use any database supported by Node JS.

There are many popular options, including **SQLite**, PostgreSQL, MySQL, Redis, and MongoDB.

</Section>

<Section>

## 3. SQLite

The SQLite provides a command-line tool **sqlite3**.

It allows the user to enter and execute SQL statements against an SQLite database.

### 3.1 SQLite CLI

#### 3.1.1 Listing Existing Tables

The `.tables` command is used to get the list of tables available in the SQLite database.

#### 3.1.2 Selecting Table Data

**Syntax:** `SELECT * from <table>`

</Section>

<Section>

## 4. SQLite Methods

### 4.1 Open

The SQLite `open()` method is used to connect the database server and provides a connection object to operate on the database.

**Syntax:**

```js
open({
  filename: DATABASE_PATH,
  driver: SQLITE_DATABASE_DRIVER,
});
```

It returns a promise object. On resolving the promise object, we will get the database connection object.

### 4.2 Executing SQL Queries

SQLite package provides multiple methods to execute SQL queries on a database.

Some of them are:

- **all()**
- get()
- run()
- exec(), etc.

#### 4.2.1 all()

`db.all(SQL_QUERY);`

The `all()` method is used to get multiple rows of data.

</Section>

<Section>

### 5. SQL Third-party packages

We can use `sqlite` and `sqlite3` node packages to connect SQLite Database from Node JS.

**Installation Commands**

```Terminal
npm install sqlite --save
npm install sqlite3 --save

```

</Section>

<Section>

## 6. Connecting SQLite Database from Node JS to get the books from Goodreads Website

1. Install the SQL third-party packages `sqlite` and `sqlite3`.
2. Initialize the SQLite Database

### 6.1 SQLite Database Initialization

```js
const express = require("express");
const path = require("path");

const { open } = require("sqlite");
const sqlite3 = require("sqlite3");
const app = express();

const dbPath = path.join(__dirname, "goodreads.db");

let db = null;

const initializeDBAndServer = async () => {
  try {
    db = await open({
      filename: dbPath,
      driver: sqlite3.Database,
    });
    app.listen(3000, () => {
      console.log("Server Running at http://localhost:3000/");
    });
  } catch (e) {
    console.log(`DB Error: ${e.message}`);
    process.exit(1);
  }
};

initializeDBAndServer();
```

### 6.2 Goodreads Get Books API

```js
app.get("/books/", async (request, response) => {
  const getBooksQuery = `
    SELECT
      *
    FROM
      book
    ORDER BY
      book_id;`;
  const booksArray = await db.all(getBooksQuery);
  response.send(booksArray);
});
```

</Section>

<Section>

## 1. SQLite Methods

The SQLite package provides multiple methods to execute SQL queries on a database.

Some of them are:

- all()
- **get()**
- **run()**
- exec(), etc.

### 1.1 get()

The `get()` method is used to get a single row from the table.

**Syntax:**

`db.get(SQL_QUERY);`

### 1.2 run()

The `run()` method is used to create or update table data.

**Syntax:**

`db.run(SQL_QUERY);`

</Section>

<Section>

## 2. Node JS Third-party packages

### 2.1 Nodemon

The Nodemon is a tool that **restarts the server automatically** whenever we make changes in the file.

**Installation Command:**

```Terminal
npm install -g nodemon
```

<MultiLineNote>
- The`-g` indicates that the nodemon will be installed globally in the environment.
- While executing the file, replace the node with the nodemon. For example, `nodemon index.js`.
</MultiLineNote>

</Section>

<Section>

## 3. GoodReads APIs

- Get Book
- Add Book
- Update Book
- Delete Book
- Get Author Books

### 3.1 Get Book

We can use `/books/:bookId/` as a path to identify a single book resource, where `bookId` is a path parameter.

For example,

In `http://localhost:3000/books/1/`, the `bookId` is `1`.

```js
app.get("/books/:bookId/", async (request, response) => {
  const { bookId } = request.params;
  const getBookQuery = `
    SELECT
      *
    FROM
      book
    WHERE
      book_id = ${bookId};`;
  const book = await db.get(getBookQuery);
  response.send(book);
});
```

<MultiLineNote>Any string can be used as a path parameter.</MultiLineNote>

### 3.2 Add Book

To add a book to the Database, you need to send a request body in JSON format.

- The `express.json()` is used to recognize the incoming request object as JSON Object and parses it.

- The `request.body` is used to get the HTTP Request body.

```js
app.post("/books/", async (request, response) => {
  const bookDetails = request.body;
  const {
    title,
    authorId,
    rating,
    ratingCount,
    reviewCount,
    description,
    pages,
    dateOfPublication,
    editionLanguage,
    price,
    onlineStores,
  } = bookDetails;
  const addBookQuery = `
    INSERT INTO
      book (title,author_id,rating,rating_count,review_count,description,pages,date_of_publication,edition_language,price,online_stores)
    VALUES
      (
        '${title}',
         ${authorId},
         ${rating},
         ${ratingCount},
         ${reviewCount},
        '${description}',
         ${pages},
        '${dateOfPublication}',
        '${editionLanguage}',
         ${price},
        '${onlineStores}'
      );`;

  const dbResponse = await db.run(addBookQuery);
  const bookId = dbResponse.lastID;
  response.send({ bookId: bookId });
});
```

The `dbResponse.lastID` provides the primary key of the new row inserted.

### 3.3 Update Book

We can use `/books/:bookId/` as a path to identify a single book resource, where `:bookId` is the path parameter.

For example, `http://localhost:3000/books/1/`.

```js
app.put("/books/:bookId/", async (request, response) => {
  const { bookId } = request.params;
  const bookDetails = request.body;
  const {
    title,
    authorId,
    rating,
    ratingCount,
    reviewCount,
    description,
    pages,
    dateOfPublication,
    editionLanguage,
    price,
    onlineStores,
  } = bookDetails;
  const updateBookQuery = `
    UPDATE
      book
    SET
      title='${title}',
      author_id=${authorId},
      rating=${rating},
      rating_count=${ratingCount},
      review_count=${reviewCount},
      description='${description}',
      pages=${pages},
      date_of_publication='${dateOfPublication}',
      edition_language='${editionLanguage}',
      price=${price},
      online_stores='${onlineStores}'
    WHERE
      book_id = ${bookId};`;
  await db.run(updateBookQuery);
  response.send("Book Updated Successfully");
});
```

The `request.params` provides the parameters passed through the request.

<MultiLineNote>The strings sent through the APIs must be wrapped in quotes.</MultiLineNote>

### 3.4 Delete Book

```js
app.delete("/books/:bookId/", async (request, response) => {
  const { bookId } = request.params;
  const deleteBookQuery = `
    DELETE FROM
      book
    WHERE
      book_id = ${bookId};`;
  await db.run(deleteBookQuery);
  response.send("Book Deleted Successfully");
});
```

### 3.5 Get Author Books

```js
app.get("/authors/:authorId/books/", async (request, response) => {
  const { authorId } = request.params;
  const getAuthorBooksQuery = `
    SELECT
     *
    FROM
     book
    WHERE
      author_id = ${authorId};`;
  const booksArray = await db.all(getAuthorBooksQuery);
  response.send(booksArray);
});
```

## 1. Get Books API

Let's see how to add **Filters** to **Get Books API**

### 1.1 Filtering Books

- Get a specific number of books
- Get books based on search query text
- Get books in the sorted order

### 1.1.1 Get a specific number of books

To get specific number of books in certain range we use **limit** and **offset**.

**Offset** is used to specify the **position** from where rows are to be selected.

**Limit** is used to specify the **number of rows**.
and many more...
Query parameters starts with **?** (question mark) followed by key value pairs separated by **&** (ampersand)

**Example :**

```http
http://localhost:3000/books/?limit=2

http://localhost:3000/books/?offset=2&limit=3

http://localhost:3000/authors/20/books/?offset=2

```

<MultiLineNote>
- The query parameters are used to sort/filter resources.

- The path parameters are used to identify a specific resource(s)
  </MultiLineNote>

### 1.1.2 Get books based on search query text

We provide query text to **search_q** key

```http
search_q = potter
```

### 1.1.3 Get books in the sorted order

We provide sorted order to **order** key

Ascending: **ASC**
DESCENDING: **DESC**

```http
order = ASC
order = DESC
```

### Filtering GET Books API

```js
app.get("/books/", async (request, response) => {
  const {
    offset = 2,
    limit = 5,
    order = "ASC",
    order_by = "book_id",
    search_q = "",
  } = request.query;
  const getBooksQuery = `
    SELECT
      *
    FROM
     book
    WHERE
     title LIKE '%${search_q}%'
    ORDER BY ${order_by} ${order}
    LIMIT ${limit} OFFSET ${offset};`;
  const booksArray = await db.all(getBooksQuery);
  response.send(booksArray);
});
```

<MultiLineNote>
We can skip or add slash while appending query parameters to the URL

`http://localhost:3000/books/?offset=2&limit=3` is same as `http://localhost:3000/books?offset=2&limit=3`
</MultiLineNote>

## 2. REST APIs

**REST**: Representational State Transfer

REST is a **set of principles** that define how Web standards, such as HTTP and URLs, are supposed to be used.

### 2.1 Why Rest Principles?

Using Rest Principles improves application in various aspects like **scalability**, **reliability** etc

### 2.2 REST API Principles

- Providing unique ID to each resource
- Using standard methods like GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE
- Accept and Respond with JSON

  and many more...

<Section>

## 1. Installing Third-party package bcrypt

Storing the passwords in plain text within a database is not a good idea since they can be misused, So Passwords should be encrypted

`bcrypt` package provides **functions** to perform operations like **encryption**, **comparison**, etc

- **bcrypt.hash()** uses various processes and encrypts the given password and makes it unpredictable
- **bcrypt.compare()** function compares the password entered by the user and hash against each other

**Installation Command**

```terminal
root@123:~/myapp# npm install bcrypt --save
```

</Section>

<Section>

## 2. Goodreads APIs for Specified Users

We need to maintain the list of users in a table and provide access only to that specified users

User needs to be registered and then log in to access the books

- Register User API
- Login User API

### 2.1 Register User API

Here we check whether the user is a new user or an existing user.
Returns "User Already exists" for existing user else for a new user we store encrypted password in the DB

```js
app.post("/users/", async (request, response) => {
  const { username, name, password, gender, location } = request.body;
  const hashedPassword = await bcrypt.hash(request.body.password, 10);
  const selectUserQuery = `SELECT * FROM user WHERE username = '${username}'`;
  const dbUser = await db.get(selectUserQuery);
  if (dbUser === undefined) {
    const createUserQuery = `
      INSERT INTO 
        user (username, name, password, gender, location) 
      VALUES 
        (
          '${username}', 
          '${name}',
          '${hashedPassword}', 
          '${gender}',
          '${location}'
        )`;
    const dbResponse = await db.run(createUserQuery);
    const newUserId = dbResponse.lastID;
    response.send(`Created new user with ${newUserId}`);
  } else {
    response.status = 400;
    response.send("User already exists");
  }
});
```

### 2.2 Login User API

Here we check whether the user exists in DB or not.
Returns "Invalid User" if the user doesn't exist else we compare the given password with the DB user password

```js
app.post("/login", async (request, response) => {
  const { username, password } = request.body;
  const selectUserQuery = `SELECT * FROM user WHERE username = '${username}'`;
  const dbUser = await db.get(selectUserQuery);
  if (dbUser === undefined) {
    response.status(400);
    response.send("Invalid User");
  } else {
    const isPasswordMatched = await bcrypt.compare(password, dbUser.password);
    if (isPasswordMatched === true) {
      response.send("Login Success!");
    } else {
      response.status(400);
      response.send("Invalid Password");
    }
  }
});
```

**Status Codes**

| Status Codes | Status Text ID    |
| ------------ | ----------------- |
| 200          | OK                |
| 204          | No Response       |
| 301          | Moved Permanently |
| 400          | Bad Request       |
| 403          | Forbidden         |
| 401          | Unauthorized      |

<Section>

## 1. Authentication Mechanisms

To check whether the user is logged in or not we use different Authentication mechanisms

Commonly used Authentication mechanisms:

- Token Authentication
- Session Authentication

## 2. Token Authentication mechanism

We use the Access Token to verify whether the user is logged in or not

### 2.1 Access Token

Access Token is a set of characters which are used to identify a user

**Example:**

It is used to **verify** whether a user is Valid/Invalid

### 2.2 How Token Authentication works?

- Server generates token and certifies the client
- Client uses this token on every subsequent request
- Client don’t need to provide entire details every time

## 3. JWT

**JSON Web Token** is a standard used to create access tokens for an application
This access token can also be called as JWT Token

### 3.1 How JWT works?

**Client:** Login with username and password
**Server:** Returns a JWT Token
**Client:** Sends JWT Token while requesting
**Server:** Sends Response to the client

### 3.2 JWT Package

`jsonwebtoken` package provides `jwt.sign` and `jwt.verify` functions

- `jwt.sign()` function takes payload, secret key, options as arguments and generates JWTToken out of it
- `jwt.verify()` verifies jwtToken and if it’s valid, returns payload. Else, it throws an error

```terminal
root@123root@123:.../myapp# npm install jsonwebtoken
```

## 4. Login User API by generating the JWT Token

When the user tries to log in, verify the Password.
Returns **JWT Token** if the password matches else return **Invalid Password** with status code **400**.

```js
app.post("/login", async (request, response) => {
  const { username, password } = request.body;
  const selectUserQuery = `SELECT * FROM user WHERE username = '${username}'`;
  const dbUser = await db.get(selectUserQuery);
  if (dbUser === undefined) {
    response.status(400);
    response.send("Invalid User");
  } else {
    const isPasswordMatched = await bcrypt.compare(password, dbUser.password);
    if (isPasswordMatched === true) {
      const payload = {
        username: username,
      };
      const jwtToken = jwt.sign(payload, "MY_SECRET_TOKEN");
      response.send({ jwtToken });
    } else {
      response.status(400);
      response.send("Invalid Password");
    }
  }
});
```

## 5. How to pass JWT Token?

We have to add an authorization header to our request and the JWT Token is passed as a **Bearer token**

```terminal
GET http://localhost:3000/books?offset=2&limit=3&search_q=the&order_by=price&order=DESC
Authorization: bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJuYW1lIjoicmFodWwiLCJnZW5kZXIiOiJNYWxlIiwibG9jYXRpb24iOiJoeWRlcmFiYWQiLCJpYXQiOjE2MTc0MzI0MDd9.Eqevw5QE70ZAVrmOZUc6pflUbeI0ffZUmQLDHYplU8g
```

## 6. Get Books API with Token Authentication

Here we check for JWT Token from Headers.
If JWT Token is not present it returns an **Invalid Access Token** with status code **401** else **verify** the **JWT Token**.

```js
app.get("/books/", (request, response) => {
  let jwtToken;
  const authHeader = request.headers["authorization"];
  if (authHeader !== undefined) {
    jwtToken = authHeader.split(" ")[1];
  }
  if (jwtToken === undefined) {
    response.status(401);
    response.send("Invalid Access Token");
  } else {
    jwt.verify(jwtToken, "MY_SECRET_TOKEN", async (error, payload) => {
      if (error) {
        response.send("Invalid Access Token");
      } else {
        const getBooksQuery = `
            SELECT
              *
            FROM
             book
            ORDER BY
              book_id;`;
        const booksArray = await db.all(getBooksQuery);
        response.send(booksArray);
      }
    });
  }
});
```

<Section>

## 1. Middleware functions

Middleware is a special kind of function in Express JS which accepts the request from

- the user (or)
- the previous middleware

After processing the request the middleware function

- sends the response to another middleware (or)
- calls the API Handler (or)
- sends response to the user

```js
app.method(Path, middleware1, handler);
```

Example

```js
const jsonMiddleware = express.json();
app.use(jsonMiddleware);
```

It is a **built-in middleware function** it recognizes the incoming request object as a JSON object, parses it, and then calls handler in **every API call**

### 1.1 Multiple Middleware functions

We can pass multiple middleware functions

```js
app.method(Path, middleware1, middleware2, handler);
```

</Section>

<Section>
## 2. Logger Middleware Implementation

### 2.1 Defining a Middleware Function

```js
const middlewareFunction = (request, response, next) => {};
```

### 2.2 Logger Middleware Function

```js
const logger = (request, response, next) => {
  console.log(request.query);
  next();
};
```

The **next** parameter is a function passed by Express JS which, when **invoked**, executes the **next succeeding function**

### 2.3 Get Books API with Logger Middleware

```js
app.get("/books/", logger, async (request, response) => {
  const getBooksQuery = `
   SELECT
    *
   FROM
    book
   ORDER BY
    book_id;`;
  const booksArray = await db.all(getBooksQuery);
  response.send(booksArray);
});
```

</Section>

<Section>
## 3. Authenticate Token Middleware

In Authenticate Token Middleware we will verify the JWT Token

```js
const authenticateToken = (request, response, next) => {
  let jwtToken;
  const authHeader = request.headers["authorization"];
  if (authHeader !== undefined) {
    jwtToken = authHeader.split(" ")[1];
  }
  if (jwtToken === undefined) {
    response.status(401);
    response.send("Invalid JWT Token");
  } else {
    jwt.verify(jwtToken, "MY_SECRET_TOKEN", async (error, payload) => {
      if (error) {
        response.status(401);
        response.send("Invalid JWT Token");
      } else {
        next();
      }
    });
  }
};
```

</Section>
<Section>
## 4. Get Books API with Authenticate Token Middleware

Let's Pass Authenticate Token Middleware to Get Books API

```js
app.get("/books/", authenticateToken, async (request, response) => {
  const getBooksQuery = `
   SELECT
    *
   FROM
    book
   ORDER BY
    book_id;`;
  const booksArray = await db.all(getBooksQuery);
  response.send(booksArray);
});
```

</Section>
<Section>
## 5. Passing data from Authenticate Token Middleware

We **cannot directly pass** data to the next handler, but we can send data through the **request** object

```js
const authenticateToken = (request, response, next) => {
  let jwtToken;
  const authHeader = request.headers["authorization"];
  if (authHeader !== undefined) {
    jwtToken = authHeader.split(" ")[1];
  }
  if (jwtToken === undefined) {
    response.status(401);
    response.send("Invalid JWT Token");
  } else {
    jwt.verify(jwtToken, "MY_SECRET_TOKEN", async (error, payload) => {
      if (error) {
        response.status(401);
        response.send("Invalid JWT Token");
      } else {
        request.username = payload.username;
        next();
      }
    });
  }
};
```

</Section>
<Section>
## 6. Get User Profile API with Authenticate Token Middleware

We can access request variable from the request object

```js
app.get("/profile/", authenticateToken, async (request, response) => {
  let { username } = request;
  const selectUserQuery = `SELECT * FROM user WHERE username = '${username}'`;
  const userDetails = await db.get(selectUserQuery);
  response.send(userDetails);
});
```

</Section>

unit_title: Connecting MongoDB to Node JS | Reading Material
content:
# Connecting MongoDB to Node.js

Integrating MongoDB with Node.js involves connecting a MongoDB database to your Node.js application. This enables efficient data management and storage for modern backend applications.

### **Why Integrate MongoDB with Node.js?**

1. **Seamless Data Handling:**
   - MongoDB uses a **document-based data structure** (similar to JSON).
   - This format aligns naturally with **JavaScript** in Node.js, making data handling simpler.
   - MongoDB's flexible JSON-like format allows easy scaling without needing fixed tables.

2. **Performance & Scalability:**
   - **Node.js** is event-driven and non-blocking, making it great for handling real-time applications.
   - **MongoDB** scales well, making it ideal for applications like **chat systems**, **IoT platforms**, and **real-time dashboards**.

3. **Developer Productivity:**
   - MongoDB's **NoSQL** approach is natural for **JavaScript developers**, eliminating the need to switch between paradigms (SQL vs NoSQL).
   - Working with **Node.js** and **MongoDB** allows a more efficient development process with fewer context switches.

4. **Modern Tech Fit:**
   - MongoDB is part of the **MEAN** and **MERN** tech stacks, widely used in modern web development.
   - It's **cloud-native**, with easy deployment and management through **MongoDB Atlas** and tools like **MongoDB Compass**.

---

### **Creating a Server using Express**

To build the backend, we first need to set up a **Node.js Express server** that will serve as the foundation for our MongoDB connection.

1. **Setting Up Express Server:**

   ```js
   const express = require("express");
   const app = express();
   const PORT = 3000;

   app.listen(PORT, () => {
     console.log(`Server started and running at ${PORT}`);
   });
```

* This code sets up a basic **Express server** on port `3000`.
* The server will handle API requests, including those that interact with the MongoDB database.

2. **Creating MongoDB Connection String:**

   * MongoDB Atlas provides a **connection string** that will be used to connect the database to the application.

---

### **Setting Up MongoDB Atlas**

MongoDB Atlas is a cloud service that makes it easy to deploy and manage MongoDB clusters. Here's how to set it up:

1. **Create a MongoDB Cluster:**

   * Go to [MongoDB Atlas](https://www.mongodb.com/products/platform/cloud) and log in.
   * Click **+ New Project**, provide a name, and click **Create Project**.
   * Set up a **Free Cluster**, and note the **username** and **password**.

2. **Get the Connection String:**

 
   
   * After setting up the cluster,click on **connect** tab and then  go to the **Drivers** tab to get the **connection string**.
   * This string will be used to connect your Node.js application to MongoDB.


3. **Configuring Network Access in MongoDB Atlas **

   * To configure network access in MongoDB Atlas, go to the **Network Access** option on the left-hand menu.
   * Then, click on **Add IP Address**, select **Allow Access From Anywhere**, and finally, click on **Confirm**. 
   * This setup ensures that your cluster can be accessed from any IP address without restrictions.


---

### **Connect Local Server to MongoDB**

Now that MongoDB is set up, let’s connect it to our Express server.

1. **Installing MongoDB in IDE:**

   * Install the `mongodb` package in your Node.js project:

     ```
     npm install mongodb
     ```

2. **Importing MongoClient:**

   * Import `MongoClient` from the `mongodb` package to interact with MongoDB:

   ```js
   const { MongoClient } = require('mongodb');
   ```
   * MongoClient is a primary class for interacting with a MongoDB server

  

   #### Uses of MongoClient in MongoDB

The **MongoClient** in MongoDB is used to handle various database operations. It helps to **establish connections** with the MongoDB server and efficiently **manage those connections**. Through MongoClient, you can **access databases and collections**, making it easier to work with structured data. Additionally, it allows you to perform **CRUD operations** (Create, Read, Update, Delete) to interact with and manage the stored data.


---

### **Using .env File for Secure Configuration**

To keep sensitive data safe (like MongoDB credentials), use the **dotenv** package to store configuration in environment variables.



1. **Why Use dotenv:**

   * **Security:** Keeps sensitive data like **database credentials** out of the code.
   * **Configuration Flexibility:** Easily switch between different environments (development, testing, production).
   * **Clean Code:** Avoids hardcoding credentials in the application, ensuring cleaner code.


2. **Install dotenv:**

   * Run the following command to install dotenv:

     ```
     npm install dotenv
     ```

3. **Create the `.env` File:**

   * In the root of your project, create a `.env` file with the MongoDB connection string:

     ```
     MONGODB_URL=mongodb+srv://<username>:<password>@cluster0.mongodb.net/mydb
     ```

---

### **Connecting to MongoDB Using dotenv**

Now, we’ll modify the Express server to securely use the connection string from the `.env` file.

1. **Modifying the Server Configuration:**

   ```js
   const express = require('express');
   const dotenv = require('dotenv');
   const { MongoClient } = require('mongodb');

   dotenv.config();  // Load the environment variables

   MongoClient.connect(process.env.MONGODB_URL)
     .then(() => {
       console.log('DB Connected Successfully');
     })
     .catch((error) => {
       console.log('Error', error);
     });

   const app = express();
   app.listen(3000, () => {
     console.log('Server started');
   });
   ```

   * `dotenv.config()` loads environment variables from the `.env` file, allowing secure access to the **MongoDB URL**.
   * The **MongoDB connection string** is accessed through `process.env.MONGODB_URL` for security.
   * **MongoClient.connect()** is used to establish the connection with MongoDB.

---

### **Summary of Steps:**

1. Set up the **Express server** in Node.js.
2. Create a **MongoDB Atlas** account and configure a cluster.
3. Install the necessary packages, like **mongodb** and **dotenv**.
4. Securely store your **MongoDB credentials** using a `.env` file.
5. Establish a connection to MongoDB using **MongoClient** and your connection string.


With these steps, you have successfully integrated MongoDB with your Node.js application and can start building out more features and database interactions.

unit_title: Introduction to MVC and Mongoose Models | Reading Material
content:
# Introduction to Mongoose

## 1. Replacing MongoDB Native Driver with Mongoose

### 1.1 What is Mongoose?
Mongoose is a popular **Object Data Modeling (ODM)** library for MongoDB and Node.js. It simplifies database interactions by allowing developers to use JavaScript objects instead of writing raw database queries.

- **ODM (Object Data Modeling)** bridges object-oriented programming (JavaScript/Node.js) and document databases (MongoDB).
- Instead of writing raw MongoDB queries like:


```js
db.users.insertOne({ name: "Priya", age: 25 });
```

With Mongoose, you define a schema and use a model:

```js
const user = new User({ name: "Priya", age: 25 });
user.save();
```

### 1.2 Benefits of Mongoose
- Provides a structured way to define models and schemas.
- Automatically maps JavaScript objects into MongoDB queries internally.
- Simplifies CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete).

---

## 2. Installing Mongoose

### 2.1 Installation Command
Run the following command in your terminal to install Mongoose:

```bash
npm install mongoose
```

---

## 3. Initial Setup

### 3.1 Connecting to MongoDB Using Mongoose
Replace the native MongoDB driver (`MongoClient`) with Mongoose for easier database interaction.

#### Code Example:
```js
const express = require('express');
const dotEnv = require('dotenv');
const mongoose = require('mongoose');

const app = express();
dotEnv.config();

mongoose.connect(process.env.MONGODB_URL).then(() => {
  console.log("DB Connected Successfully");
}).catch((error) => {
  console.log("Error", error);
});

app.listen(3000, () => {
  console.log("Server started");
});
```

### 3.2 Key Differences
- **MongoClient**: Low-level, official MongoDB driver.
- **Mongoose**: Built on top of MongoDB driver, adds structure and features.

---

# Introduction to MVC Architecture

## 4. What is MVC?

### 4.1 Overview
MVC stands for **Model-View-Controller**, a common design pattern that organizes web applications into clear layers:

- **Model**: Represents the data and business logic related to the database.
- **View**: Responsible for rendering the user interface (UI).
- **Controller**: Acts as a middleman between the Model and the View, handling application logic.

### 4.2 Benefits of MVC
1. **Better Separation of Concerns**:
   - Model → Data
   - View → UI
   - Controller → Logic

2. **Easier Testing and Debugging**:
   - Logic, UI, and database code are separated for independent testing.

3. **Scalability for Larger Applications**:
   - Modify one layer without affecting others.
   - Multiple developers can work on different parts simultaneously.

---

## 5. CRUD Operations in E-Commerce

### 5.1 CRUD Overview
CRUD stands for **Create, Read, Update, Delete**. These operations are essential for managing data in applications.

### 5.2 Entities in E-Commerce
#### User Entity:
Fields required:

- Name
- Email
- Password
- Role
- isActive
- createdAt

#### Product Entity:
Fields required:

- Name
- Category
- Price
- Description
- Stock
- createdAt

---

# Creating Schema and Model

## 6. Schema

### 6.1 What is a Schema?
A **Schema** defines the structure of documents in a MongoDB collection. It specifies:

- Fields
- Data types
- Validations
- Default values

### 6.2 Creating a User Schema
1. Create a folder named `models`.
2. Inside the folder, create a file named `userModel.js`.

#### Code Example:

```js
const mongoose = require('mongoose');

const userSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  name: {
    type: String,
    required: true,
    minlength: 3
  },
  email: {
    type: String,
    required: true,
    unique: true,
    lowercase: true,
    trim: true
  },
  password: {
    type: String,
    required: true,
    minlength: 6
  },
  role: {
    type: String,
    enum: ['user', 'admin'],
    default: 'user'
  },
  isActive: {
    type: Boolean,
    default: true
  },
  createdAt: {
    type: Date,
    default: Date.now
  }
});

module.exports = mongoose.model('User', userSchema);
```

### 6.3 Explanation of Fields

- **name**: String, required, minimum length of 3 characters.
- **email**: String, required, unique, stored in lowercase, trimmed.
- **password**: String, required, minimum length of 6 characters.
- **role**: String, can be either 'user' or 'admin', default is 'user'.
- **isActive**: Boolean, default is true.
- **createdAt**: Date, default is the current date and time.

---

## 7. Model

### 7.1 What is a Model?
A **Model** is created from a schema and provides an interface to interact with the database. It allows:

- Creating new documents.
- Querying existing documents.
- Updating documents.
- Deleting documents.

#### Code Example:

```js
const mongoose = require('mongoose');

const userSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  name: { ... },
  email: { ... },
  password: { ... },
  role: { ... },
  isActive: { ... },
  createdAt: {
    type: Date,
    default: Date.now
  }
});

module.exports = mongoose.model('User', userSchema);
```

---

## 8. Product Schema

### 8.1 Creating a Product Schema
1. Create a file named `productModel.js` inside the `models` folder.

#### Code Example:
```js
const mongoose = require('mongoose');

const productSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  name: {
    type: String,
    required: true
  },
  category: {
    type: String,
    required: true
  },
  price: {
    type: Number,
    required: true
  },
  description: {
    type: String
  },
  stock: {
    type: Number,
    required: true
  },
  createdAt: {
    type: Date,
    default: Date.now
  }
});

module.exports = mongoose.model('Product', productSchema);
```

### 8.2 Explanation of Fields

- **name**: String, required.
- **category**: String, required.
- **price**: Number, required.
- **description**: String, optional.
- **stock**: Number, required.
- **createdAt**: Date, default is the current date and time.

---

content:
# Implementing CRUD Operations in Node.js

## 1. Creating the Controller

### 1.1 Setting Up the Controller
- Create a new folder called `controllers`.
- Inside it, create a file named `userController.js`.

```plaintext
controllers/
  userController.js
```

### 1.2 Importing the User Model
- Import the `User` model into the `userController.js` file.

```js
const User = require('../models/User');
```

### 1.3 Implementing the `createUser` Controller
- Define a function `createUser` to handle the creation of new users.

```js
const createUser = async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const { name, email, password } = req.body;

    const user = new User({
      name,
      email,
      password
    });

    const savedUser = await user.save();
    res.status(201).json(savedUser);
  } catch (err) {
    res.status(400).json({ message: err.message });
  }
};
```

### 1.4 Exporting the Controller
- Export the `createUser` function for use in other parts of the application.

```js
module.exports = { createUser };
```

---

## 2. Setting Up the Routes

### 2.1 Creating the Routes File
- Create a new folder called `routes`.
- Inside it, create a file named `userRoutes.js`.

```plaintext
routes/
  userRoutes.js
```

### 2.2 Importing Dependencies
- Import `express` and create a router instance.

```js
const express = require('express');
const router = express.Router();
```

### 2.3 Importing the Controller
- Import the `createUser` controller from `userController.js`.

```js
const { createUser } = require('../controllers/userController');
```

### 2.4 Defining the POST Route
- Define a POST route `/add-user` that uses the `createUser` controller.

```js
router.post('/add-user', createUser);
```

### 2.5 Exporting the Router
- Export the router instance for use in the application.

```js
module.exports = router;
```

---

## 3. Connecting Routes to the Server

### 3.1 Setting Up the Server
- Create a `server.js` file and set up the basic server configuration.

```js
const express = require('express');
const dotEnv = require('dotenv');
const mongoose = require('mongoose');

const app = express();

dotEnv.config();
mongoose.connect(process.env.MONGODB_URL);

app.listen(3000, () => {
  console.log('Server started');
});
```

### 3.2 Importing Routes
- Import the `userRoutes` into the `server.js` file.

```js
const userRoutes = require('./routes/userRoutes');
```

### 3.3 Enabling JSON Parsing
- Enable the application to parse incoming JSON request bodies.

```js
app.use(express.json());
```

### 3.4 Connecting Routes
- Connect the `userRoutes` to the `/users` endpoint.

```js
app.use('/users', userRoutes);

const PORT = 3000;
app.listen(PORT, () => {
  console.log(`Server started at ${PORT}`);
});
```

---

## 4. Testing the API

### 4.1 Using Postman
- Postman is a powerful tool for API testing. Download and install it from [Postman Downloads](https://www.postman.com/downloads/).

### 4.2 Testing the POST Route

1. Open Postman and create a new request.
2. Set the request type to `POST`.
3. Enter the URL: `{Your_Domain_URL}/users/add-user`.

#### Headers
- Go to the "Headers" tab and add the following header:
  - **Key:** `Content-Type`
  - **Value:** `application/json`

#### Body
- Go to the "Body" tab, select `raw` and `JSON`, and enter the following JSON:

```json
{
  "name": "John Doe",
  "email": "john@example.com",
  "password": "securePass123"
}
```

4. Click the **Send** button.
5. You should receive a `201 Created` response with the saved user data.

---

## 5. Verifying Data in MongoDB

### 5.1 Checking the Database
- Log in to MongoDB on your browser.
- Open the project and navigate to "Browse Collections" to verify the user details.

---

## 6. Implicit Database Creation in MongoDB

### 6.1 Default Database Creation
- MongoDB automatically creates a default database named `test` when data is added without specifying a database name.

### 6.2 Specifying a Custom Database
- To use a specific database, include the database name in the connection string.

```plaintext
mongodb+srv://root:root@cluster0.kieayrs.mongodb.net/db_name?retryWrites=true&w=majority&appName=Cluster0
```

### 6.3 Example Connection String
- Define the database name in the `.env` file.

```plaintext
MONGODB_URL=mongodb+srv://root:root@cluster0.kieayrs.mongodb.net/ecommerce?retryWrites=true&w=majority&appName=Cluster0
```

content:
# Filtering and Sorting in E-Commerce Applications

## 1. Introduction to Filtering and Sorting

Filtering and sorting are essential features in e-commerce applications like Amazon and Flipkart. They allow users to refine search results based on specific criteria such as price range, category, and name, and sort the results in a desired order.

---

## 2. Filtering and Sorting API

### 2.1 Example Use Case

Imagine you are searching for "Samsung headphones" on an e-commerce platform. You:

- Set a price range: ₹500 to ₹2,000
- Pick a category: Electronics
- Sort by: Price Low to High
- Browse page 2 of results

This process involves:

- **Filtering** by price range and category
- **Searching** by name
- **Sorting** by price

---

## 3. SQLite Recap

### 3.1 Key SQLite Concepts
- **Filtering Rows**: Use the `WHERE` clause to filter rows based on conditions.
- **Sorting Results**: Use `ORDER BY` to sort results in ascending or descending order.
- **Pagination**: Use `LIMIT` and `OFFSET` to control the number of results displayed.
- **Extracting Query Parameters**: Use `request.query` to extract values from the URL.

### 3.2 Example Query

```sql
SELECT * FROM product
WHERE price >= 500 AND price <= 2000
ORDER BY price ASC
LIMIT 5 OFFSET 10;
````

---

## 4. Filtering and Sorting in Mongoose

### 4.1 Key Differences Between SQL and Mongoose

* **SQL**: Uses text commands (e.g., `WHERE`, `ORDER BY`).
* **Mongoose**: Uses JavaScript objects for queries (e.g., `.find()`, `.sort()`).

### 4.2 Filtering and Sorting in Mongoose

* **Filter Products**: Create an object with conditions and pass it to `.find()`.
* **Sort Products**: Create an object with sorting rules and pass it to `.sort()`.

---

## 5. Implementing Filters

### 5.1 Filtering by Price Range

#### 5.1.1 Adding Min and Max Price Conditions

```js
const filter = {};
if (req.query.minPrice) {
  filter.price = { ...filter.price, $gte: Number(req.query.minPrice) };
}
if (req.query.maxPrice) {
  filter.price = { ...filter.price, $lte: Number(req.query.maxPrice) };
}
const products = await Product.find(filter);
```

#### 5.1.2 Testing Price Range Filter

* **Test 1**: `GET /products?minPrice=500`
  Expected: Products priced above ₹500.

* **Test 2**: `GET /products?maxPrice=1000`
  Expected: Products priced below ₹1000.

* **Test 3**: `GET /products?minPrice=500&maxPrice=1000`
  Expected: Products priced between ₹500 and ₹1000.

---

### 5.2 Filtering by Category

#### 5.2.1 Exact Match Filtering

```js
if (req.query.category) {
  filter.category = req.query.category;
}
```

#### 5.2.2 Example

* **Request**: `GET /products?category=Electronics`
* **Resulting Filter**: `{ category: "Electronics" }`

---

### 5.3 Searching by Name

#### 5.3.1 Using `$regex` for Partial Matches

```js
if (req.query.search) {
  filter.name = { $regex: req.query.search, $options: "i" };
}
```

#### 5.3.2 Example

* **Request**: `GET /products?search=samsung`
* **Database**: `"Samsung Galaxy"`, `"Samsung Mobile Case"`, `"Laptop Stand"`
* **Result**: Matches `"Samsung Galaxy"` and `"Samsung Mobile Case"`, excludes `"Laptop Stand"`.

---

## 6. Sorting

### 6.1 Adding Sorting to the Query

```js
let sortObj = {};
if (req.query.sortBy) {
  const order = req.query.order === "desc" ? -1 : 1;
  sortObj[req.query.sortBy] = order;
}
const products = await Product.find(filter).sort(sortObj);
```

### 6.2 Example

* **Request**: `GET /products?sortBy=price&order=asc`
* **Result**: Products sorted by price in ascending order.

---

## 7. Combining Filters and Sorting

### 7.1 Complete Controller Code

```js
const getAllProducts = async (req, res) => {
  try {
    // 1. Build Filter Object
    const filter = {};
    if (req.query.minPrice) {
      filter.price = { ...filter.price, $gte: Number(req.query.minPrice) };
    }
    if (req.query.maxPrice) {
      filter.price = { ...filter.price, $lte: Number(req.query.maxPrice) };
    }
    if (req.query.category) {
      filter.category = req.query.category;
    }
    if (req.query.search) {
      filter.name = { $regex: req.query.search, $options: "i" };
    }

    // 2. Build Sort Object
    let sortObj = {};
    if (req.query.sortBy) {
      const order = req.query.order === "desc" ? -1 : 1;
      sortObj[req.query.sortBy] = order;
    }

    // 3. Execute Query
    const products = await Product.find(filter).sort(sortObj);

    // 4. Send Response
    res.status(200).json({
      count: products.length,
      products,
    });
  } catch (err) {
    res.status(500).json({ message: err.message });
  }
};
```

---

## 8. Testing the Complete API

### 8.1 Example Requests

1. **Search + Category + Price Range + Sort**

   `GET /products?search=samsung&category=Electronics&minPrice=500&maxPrice=2000&sortBy=price&order=asc`

2. **Category + Sort**

   `GET /products?category=Electronics&sortBy=price&order=desc`

3. **Default Behavior**

   `GET /products`

   Expected: All products, no filters or sorting applied.

---

## 9. Pagination

### 9.1 The Problem with Returning Everything

Imagine your database has 10,000 products. Returning all of them in one response leads to:

* **Slow response** — large JSON payload takes time to transmit
* **High memory usage** — server loads all 10,000 documents into memory
* **Poor user experience** — users cannot effectively browse such a large list


### 9.2 Solution:

Return results in smaller chunks (pages), for example, 10 products per request.


### 9.3 Analogy

Pagination is like reading a book. You don’t read all 300 pages at once — you read one page at a time.

* **Page number** → tells where you are
* **LIMIT** → number of items per page
* **OFFSET / SKIP** → how many items to skip before starting


### 9.4 SQLITE vs Mongoose

### “In SQLite, how did we fetch data in batches?”

We were Using `LIMIT` and `OFFSET`.

But,

### “MongoDB uses the same concept but with different methods: `.limit()` and `.skip()`.”

| SQLite               | Mongoose              |
| -------------------- | --------------------- |
| `LIMIT 10`           | `.limit(10)`          |
| `OFFSET 20`          | `.skip(20)`           |
| `LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20` | `.skip(20).limit(10)` |


### 9.5 IMPLEMENTING PAGE-BASED PAGINATION

#### 9.5.1 Converting Page Number to skip and limit

```js
const page = parseInt(req.query.page, 10) || 1;
const limit = parseInt(req.query.limit, 10) || 10;
const skip = (page - 1) * limit;

const products = await Product.find(filter)
  .sort(sortObj)
  .skip(skip)
  .limit(limit);
```


### 9.6 Math Table

| Page | Limit | Skip = (page - 1) * limit | Products Returned |
| ---- | ----- | ------------------------- | ----------------- |
| 1    | 10    | 0                         | Products 1–10     |
| 2    | 10    | 10                        | Products 11–20    |
| 3    | 10    | 20                        | Products 21–30    |
| 1    | 5     | 0                         | Products 1–5      |
| 3    | 5     | 10                        | Products 11–15    |

### 9.7 Defaults

If `page` or `limit` is not provided:

* `page = 1`
* `limit = 10`

This ensures the API works even without pagination parameters.

### 9.8 PAGINATION — LIVE DEMO

#### 9.8.1 Testing Pagination

#### Tests:

* **Test 1** — First page, 5 items:
  `GET /products?page=1&limit=5`

* **Test 2** — Second page, 5 items:
  `GET /products?page=2&limit=5`

* **Test 3** — Filter + Sort + Pagination:
  `GET /products?category=Electronics&minPrice=100&sortBy=price&order=asc&page=1&limit=3`

### 9.9 Expected Output

First 3 Electronics products with price ≥ ₹100, sorted in ascending order.

---

## 10. Additional Challenges

### 10.1 Brand Filter

* **Request**: `GET /products?brand=Samsung`
* **Hint**: Use the same pattern as the category filter.

### 10.2 In-Stock Filter

* **Request**: `GET /products?inStock=true`
* **Hint**: Convert the string `"true"` to a boolean.

### 10.3 Bonus: Rating Filter

* **Request**: `GET /products?minRating=4`
* **Hint**: Use `$gte` to filter products with a rating greater than or equal to 4.

---

# Role-Based Authorization

## 1. Role-Based Authorization Overview

### 1.1 Role-Based Authentication
Role-based authentication ensures that only users with specific roles can perform certain actions. For examp

- Adding products
- Preventing unauthorized use

### 1.2 Why We Need Role-Based Authorization
Role-based authorization is essential for:

- **Security**: Limits access to specific roles, reducing risks of misuse, data leaks, and unauthorized actions.
- **Compliance**: Helps organizations meet legal and regulatory requirements by defining access to sensitive data.
- **Maintainability**: Centralizes access management, simplifying updates when users change roles or leave. This reduces administrative effort and errors, improving operational efficiency and scalability.

---

## 2. Defining User Roles

### 2.1 Existing Roles

- `user`
- `admin`

### 2.2 New Roles

- `seller`

---

## 3. Updating the User Model

### 3.1 Extending the User Schema
Update the user schema to include the new `seller` role alongside the existing `user` and `admin` roles.


```js
const userSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  role: {
    type: String,
    enum: ["user", "admin", "seller"],
    default: "user"
  },
});
module.exports = mongoose.model("User", userSchema);
```

---

## 4. Role-Based Authorization Middleware

### 4.1 What is Role-Based Authorization Middleware (RBAM)?
RBAM is middleware designed to handle role-based access control. It:

- Extracts the user role from the JWT payload (`req.user.role`).
- Compares the role against a list of allowed roles.
- Grants or denies access based on the authorization check.

### 4.2 Benefits of RBAM

- Centralizes authorization logic.
- Prevents unauthorized access.
- Keeps route handlers focused on core business functionality.

---

## 5. Creating Role Authorization Middleware

### 5.1 Role Authorization File
Create a file to store the authorization logic:

**File Path**: `middlewares/roleAuthorization.js`

### 5.2 Role Authorization Logic
The middleware works alongside authentication middleware to provide complete security.

#### 5.2.1 Import Required Library
Import the `jsonwebtoken` library.

```js
import jwt from 'jsonwebtoken';
```

#### 5.2.2 Define the `authorizeRoles` Function
The `authorizeRoles` function takes a list of allowed roles as a parameter.

```js
const authorizeRoles = (allowedRoles) => (req, res, next) => {
  if (!req.user) {
    return res
      .status(401)
      .json({ message: 'Unauthorized: No user information found' });
  }
  if (!allowedRoles.includes(req.user.role)) {
    return res
      .status(403)
      .json({ message: 'Forbidden: You do not have the required role' });
  }
  next();
};
module.exports = { authorizeRoles };
```

#### 5.2.3 Key Features of `authorizeRoles`

- **Unauthorized Access**: If `req.user` is missing, the server responds with a `401 Unauthorized` status and a JSON error message.
- **Forbidden Access**: If the user's role is not in the allowed roles, the server responds with a `403 Forbidden` status and a JSON error message.
- **Next Middleware**: Calls the next middleware or route handler if authorization is successful.

---

## 6. Integrating Role Authorization Middleware

### 6.1 Example Integration
Add the `authorizeRoles` middleware to a route, specifying the allowed roles.

**File Path**: `productRoutes.js`

```js
const authorizeRoles = require('../middlewares/roleAuthorization');

router.post('/products/add-product', authenticateToken, authorizeRoles(['seller']), createProduct);

module.exports = router;
```

---

## 7. Testing Role-Based Authorization with Postman


### 7.1 Testing Steps in Postman

#### 7.1.1 Configure Authorization Header

- Open the Headers tab for the `POST /products/add-product` request.
- Add a new header:
  - **Key**: `Authorization`
  - **Value**: `Bearer <Paste-Your-JWT-Here>`

#### 7.1.2 Example Request Body

```json
{
  "name": "iPhone 16 Mobile Case",
  "category": "Electronics",
  "description": "Durable and stylish mobile case.",
  "price": 300,
  "createdAt": "2026-04-02T12:00:00",
  "stock": 150
}
```

---

### 7.2 Testing Scenarios

#### 7.2.1 Valid Seller Token

- **Request**: `POST /products/add-product` with JWT token having role: `seller`.
- **Expected Status**: `201 Created`.
- **Expected Response**: JSON of the newly created product.


#### 7.2.2 Valid Token with Non-Seller Role

- **Request**: `POST /products/add-product` with JWT token having role: `buyer` (or other).
- **Expected Status**: `403 Forbidden`.
- **Expected Response**: `{ "message": "Forbidden: You do not have the required role" }`.


#### 7.2.3 Missing Token

- **Request**: `POST /products/add-product` without an Authorization header.
- **Expected Status**: `401 Unauthorized`.
- **Expected Response**: `{ "message": "Access Denied: No Token Provided" }` (from `authenticateToken`).


#### 7.2.4 Expired Token

- **Request**: `POST /products/add-product` with an invalid or expired JWT.
- **Expected Status**: `401 Unauthorized` or `403 Forbidden` (depending on token verification).
- **Expected Response**: 
  - `{ "message": "Token Expired" }` or 
  - `{ "message": "Invalid Token" }`.

---

# Intro to Programming with Python

**Software**

Software is a set of instructions to the hardware.

**Programming**

Programming means writing the instructions to create a software.

**Code**

The instructions that we write to create software is called **Code**.

**Syntax**

Similar to Grammar rules in English, Hindi, each programming language has a unique set of rules. These rules are called the **Syntax** of a Programming Language.

## Why Python
Python is an easy to learn, powerful programming language. With Python, it is possible to create programs with minimal amount of code. Look at the code in Java and Python used for printing the message **"Hello World"**

**Java:**

```java
class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello World");
    }
}
```

**Python:**

```python
print("Hello World")
```

### Applications of Python

Python is a versatile language which has applications in almost every field

-   Artificial intelligence (AI)
-   Machine Learning (ML)
-   Big Data
-   Smart Devices/Internet of Things (IoT)
-   Cyber Security
-   Game Development
-   Backend Development, etc.

### Career Opportunities
Python developers have plenty of opportunities across the world

-   DevOps Engineer
-   Software Developer
-   Data Analyst
-   Data Scientist
-   Machine Learning (ML) Engineer
-   AI Scientist, etc.

## Hello World Program in Python

Here is a simple Python code that you can use to display the message **"Hello World"**

#### Code

```python
print("Hello World!")
```

#### Output


```

Hello World! 
```

### Possible Mistakes

Possible mistakes you may make while writing Python code to display the message "Hello World"

- Spelling Mistake in print 

```python
prnt("Hello World!")
```

- Uppercase ‘P’ in Print

```python
Print("Hello World!")
```

- Missing quotes 

```python
print(Hello World!)
```

- Missing parentheses

```python
print("Hello World!"
```

## Printing Without Quotes

If we want to print the numerical result of 2 + 5, we do not add quotes.

#### Code

```python
print(2 + 5) 
```
#### Output

```

7 
```
If we want to print the exact message "2 + 5", then we add the quotes.

#### Code

```python
print("2 + 5") 
```

#### Output

```

2 + 5 
```

## Calculations with python

**Addition**

Addition is denoted by `+` sign.
It gives the sum of two numbers.

#### Code

```python
print(2 + 5) 
print(1 + 1.5) 
```

#### Output

```
7 
2.5 
```

**Subtraction**

Subtraction is denoted by `-` sign.
It gives the difference between two numbers.

#### Code

```python
print(5 - 2) 
```

#### Output

```

3
```

**Multiplication**

Multiplication is denoted by `*` sign.

#### Code

```python
print(2 * 5)
print(5 * 0.5)
```

#### Output

```
10
2.5
```

**Division**

Division is denoted by `/` sign.

#### Code

```python
print(5 / 2)
print(4/2)
```

#### Output

```
2.5
2.0
```

content:
# Variables and Data Types

**Variables**

Variables are like containers for storing values.
Values in the variables can be changed.

**Values**

Consider that variables are like containers for storing information.
In context of programming, this information is often referred to as value.

## Data Type

In programming languages, every value or data has an associated type to it known as data type.
Some commonly used data types

- String
- Integer
- Float
- Boolean

This data type determines how the value or data can be used in the program. For example, mathematical operations can be done on Integer and Float types of data.

**String**

A String is a stream of characters enclosed within quotes.
Stream of Characters

- Capital Letters ( A – Z )
- Small Letters ( a – z )
- Digits ( 0 – 9 )
- Special Characters (~ ! @ # $ % ^ . ?,)
- Space


Some Examples

- `"Hello World!"`
- `"some@example.com"`
- `"1234"`

<MultiLineNote>
The Stream of characters enclosed within quotes (**Both single quotes and Double quotes**) are considered as **strings**.

* 'hello'
* "hello"
</MultiLineNote>


**Integer**

All whole numbers (positive, negative and zero) without any fractional part come under Integers.
Examples

` ...-3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3,...`


**Float**

Any number with a decimal point
`24.3, 345.210, -321.86`

**Boolean**

In a general sense, anything that can take one of two possible values is considered a Boolean. Examples include the data that can take values like 

- `True or False`
- `Yes or No`
- `0 or 1` 
- `On or Off`, etc.

As per the Python Syntax, `True` and `False` are considered as Boolean values.
Notice that both start with a capital letter.

### Assigning Value to Variable

The following is the syntax for assigning an integer value `10` to a variable `age`

```python
age = 10
```

Here the equals to `=` sign is called as **Assignment Operator** as it is used to assign values to variables.

# Sequence of Instructions

**Program**
A program is a sequence of instructions given to a computer.

## Defining a Variable
A variable gets created when you assign a value to it for the first time.

#### Code

```python
age = 10
```

### Printing Value in a Variable

#### Code

```python
age = 10
print(age)
```

#### Output

```

10
```

#### Code

```python
age = 10
print("age")
```

#### Output

```

age
```

Variable name enclosed in quotes will print variable rather than the value in it.
If you intend to print value, do not enclose the variable in quotes.

## Order of Instructions

Python executes the code line-by-line.

#### Code

```python
print(age)
age = 10
```

#### Output

```

NameError: name 'age' is not defined
```

Variable `age` is not created by the time we tried to print.

### Spacing in Python

Having spaces at the beginning of line causes errors.

#### Code

```python
a = 10 * 5
b = 5 * 0.5
 b = a + b
```

#### Output

```
File "main.py", line 3
    b = a + b
    ^
IndentationError: unexpected indent
```

### Variable Assignment
Values in the variables can be changed.

#### Code

```python
a = 1
print(a)
a = 2
print(a)
```

#### Output

```
1
2
```

**Examples of Variable Assignment**

#### Code

```python
a = 2
print(a)
a = a + 1
print(a)
```

#### Output

```
2
3
```


#### Code

```python
a = 1
b = 2
a = b + 1
print(a)
print(b)
```

#### Output

```
3
2
```


## Expression
An expression is a valid combination of values, variables and operators.

*Examples* 
    a \* b
    a + 2
    5 \* 2 + 3 \* 4


**BODMAS**
The standard order of evaluating an expression

- *Brackets* (B)
- *Orders* (O)
- *Division* (D)
- *Multiplication* (M)
- *Addition* (A)
- *Subtraction* (S)

*Step by Step Explanation*

```
(5 * 2) + (3 * 4)
(10) + (12)
22
```

#### Code

```python
print(10 / 2 + 3)
print(10 / (2 + 3))
```

#### Output

```
8.0
2.0
```

# Inputs and Outputs Basics

## Working with Strings

## String Concatenation
Joining strings together is called string concatenation.

#### Code

```python
a = "Hello" + " " + "World"
print(a)
```

#### Output

```
Hello World
```

## Concatenation Errors

String Concatenation is possible only with strings.

#### Code

```python
a = "*" + 10
print(a)
```

#### Output

```
File "main.py", line 1
    a = "*" + 10
    ^
TypeError: 
can only concatenate str (not "int") to str
```

## String Repetition

`*` operator is used for repeating strings any number of times as required.

#### Code

```python
a = "*" * 10
print(a)
```

#### Output

```
**********
```

#### Code

```python
s = "Python"
s = ("* " * 3) + s + (" *" * 3)
print(s)
```

#### Output

```
* * * Python * * *
```

## Length of String
`len()` returns the number of characters in a given string.

#### Code

```python
username = input()
length = len(username)
print(length)
```

#### Input

```
Ravi
```

#### Output

```
4
```

## Take Input From User

* `input()` allows flexibility to take the input from the user.
* input() reads a line of input as a **string**.

**Example - 1**:

#### Code

```python
username = input()
print(username)
```

#### Input

```
Ajay
```

#### Output

```
Ajay
```

**Example - 2**:

#### Code

```python
username = input()
age = input()
print(username + " is " + age + " years old")
```

#### Input

```
Ravi
10
```

#### Output

```
Ravi is 10 years old
```

<MultiLineNote>
* Even though you **can't** directly **combine strings and integers**, the code works because `input()` returns a **string**. 
* `input()` converts user-entered data (including numbers, booleans, etc) to string. You will learn more about this in further sessions.
* In this case, "10" becomes a string, allowing it to Concatenate and be the part of the final message.
</MultiLineNote>


## String Indexing

We can access an individual character in a string using their positions (which start from 0).
These positions are also called as index.

#### Code

```python
username = "Ravi"
first_letter = username[0]
print(first_letter)
```

#### Output

```
R
```

### IndexError

Attempting to use an index that is too large will result in an error:


#### Code

```python
username = "Ravi"
print(username[4])
```

#### Output

```
IndexError: string index out of range
```

# Type Conversion

## String Slicing

Obtaining a part of a string is called string slicing.

#### Code

```python
variable_name[start_index:end_index]
```

- Start from the `start_index` and stops at `end_index`
- `end_index` is not included in the slice.



#### Code

```python
message = "Hi Ravi"
part = message[3:7]
print(part)
```

#### Output

```

Ravi
```

### Slicing to End

If end index is not specified, slicing stops at the end of the string.

#### Code

```python
message = "Hi Ravi"
part = message[3:]
print(part)
```

#### Output

```

Ravi
```


### Slicing from Start

If start index is not specified, slicing starts from the index 0.

#### Code

```python
message = "Hi Ravi"
part = message[:2]
print(part)
```

#### Output

```

Hi
```

## Checking Data Type

Check the datatype of the variable or value using `type()` 

**Printing Data Type**

#### Code

```python
print(type(10))
print(type(4.2))
print(type("Hi"))
```

#### Output

```
<class 'int'>
<class 'float'>
<class 'str'>
```


## Type Conversion

Converting the value of one data type to another data type is called *Type Conversion* or *Type Casting*.
We can convert

- String to Integer
- Integer to Float
- Float to String and so on.

### String to Integer
`int()` converts valid data of any type to integer

#### Code

```python
a = "5"
a = int(a)
print(type(a))
print(a)
```

#### Output

```
<class 'int'>
5
```


### Invalid Integer Conversion

#### Code

```python
a = "Five"
a = int(a)
```

# Logical Operators

The logical operators are used to perform logical operations on Boolean values.  
Gives `True` or `False` as the result.

## Logical AND Operator
